conversation.message: I smoke bidis. About 25 a day for the last 45 years. I've reduced a little now because the doctor here keeps telling me to.
You've been smoking bidis for 45 years, that's a long time. What situations or activities seem to make you reach for a bidi more often?

When I'm stressed about something that's gone wrong on the farm. Or on slow days when there's not much work to do, it's hard to stop my mind from thinking about other things, and sometimes I need a bidi to calm down.

It sounds like stress and boredom are big challenges for you, especially on the farm when things aren't going as planned. Have you ever noticed how your body feels when you're stressed, like tense muscles or a racing heart, and could that be a signal for you to take a short break and do something else to calm down?
Yes that's right, my chest gets tight when I'm stressed. But with a bidi, the tightness goes away for a little while.
It sounds like the bidis are providing temporary relief from the chest tightness. Have you ever tried taking a few deep breaths when you feel that tightness instead of reaching for a bidi?
I tried it a few times...but it's hard to remember to do it. Bidi's just a habit, you know?

I understand that you've tried taking deep breaths before but it was hard to remember to do it. Would you be willing to try setting a reminder, like putting a note on your phone or talking to a family member about when you feel stressed to remind you to breathe deeply?
Maybe I can ask my wife to remind me when she sees me lighting up. She's always telling me to quit anyway.
That's a great idea to have your wife remind you to breathe deeply when she sees you lighting up. Does it feel more doable to you if she's the one prompting you to try this new approach?
Yes, that makes it easier. She can remind me and then I'll try to breathe. It's better than trying to do it by myself.

It sounds like having your wife's support will make it easier for you to remember to breathe deeply. How do you think you'll feel if you can manage to breathe deeply for a few minutes when you feel stressed, but then still end up having a bidi afterwards?
I don't know...I might feel a little disappointed, I guess. But maybe the doctor is right, and it's a start. If I can breathe for a little while, it's better than nothing, right?

I can see you're still feeling a bit uncertain about what to expect from this new approach. Can you imagine what it would be like if you were able to breathe deeply for a few minutes when you feel stressed, and then didn't have a bidi afterwards?
Actually, yes...I can imagine it. I'd feel a little proud of myself, I think. And maybe the tightness in my chest wouldn't be as bad. That would be nice.

It sounds like you're feeling a sense of hope and possibility about being able to manage your stress with deep breathing. Can you see yourself incorporating deep breathing into your daily routine, such as right after waking up or during your breaks at work?
Yes...the morning is hard to resist. But I might try breathing then too. See how it goes.

It sounds like you're feeling a bit more hopeful about this new approach to managing your morning cravings. Would you be open to trying a 10-minute delay technique when you feel the urge to smoke in the morning, where you tell yourself you can smoke in 10 minutes if you still want to, but do something else for those 10 minutes instead?
I don't know...10 minutes seems like a long time. But maybe I can try doing something else for 5 minutes and then see.
You're hesitant about the 10-minute delay, but willing to try something shorter. Would you consider starting with a 5-minute delay today, just to see how it feels, and we can adjust from there?
Okay, I'll try it. 5 minutes sounds doable.
It takes a lot of courage to try something new, and I wish you all the best with taking this first step.
I'll try to remember to breathe.
You mentioned remembering to breathe. Can you tell me, what do you think might help you remember to breathe deeply when you feel the urge to smoke, especially during stressful moments?
I smoke bidis. One packet a day, about 15 to 16. I've been doing this since I was around 18 or 20.
You've been smoking bidis for quite a while, so that's a lot of nicotine in your system. Can you think of times of the day or week when you usually smoke a bidi, like when you wake up or at work?
It's mostly when I'm with my coworkers at the construction site. They always have bidis and offer me one, saying "have one, yaar". Makes it hard to say no, you know?

It sounds like your coworkers make it really tough for you to resist smoking with their friendly offers. What do you think would happen if you said no to one of their offers - would they be surprised or understanding?
They might be a bit surprised at first, but they'd probably just laugh and say "come on, just one bidi". They won't understand why I'd want to stop.

It sounds like your coworkers can be friendly but also a bit persuasive when it comes to smoking bidis. Have you thought about what you would say to them if they offered you a bidi, something that would help them understand your decision to quit?
Not really... I mean, I haven't thought about trying to explain it to them. It's just something we do, you know? I'll think about it, but it might be hard to explain.

You're already thinking about how to handle these social situations, which is a great first step. Would you be open to writing down some reasons why quitting smoking is important to you, so you can refer to them when you're around your coworkers and they offer you a bidi?
I don't know... it sounds like something that might not make a difference. What's the point of writing it down if I'm just gonna end up smoking anyway?

You're feeling a bit skeptical about writing down your reasons for quitting, wondering if it'll really make a difference. What if writing down those reasons helped you feel more confident in your decision, like you're holding onto a clear goal?
I guess that's not a bad idea. But what's the benefit of feeling confident? What if I still end up smoking?

You're still a bit uncertain about whether feeling more confident in your decision to quit will actually help you stick to it. Do you think feeling confident in your decision might also make it easier to handle those moments when your coworkers offer you a bidi, even if you're still tempted to smoke?

I suppose it could make it a bit easier. But it's still just a bidi, and it's not like it's gonna solve all my problems, is it? I mean, my wife's still got a lot of stress with the household expenses...

You're feeling a bit overwhelmed by your wife's stress with the household expenses, and that's making it harder for you to focus on your own goal of quitting smoking. What if, instead of trying to tackle all those expenses at once, you and your wife made a plan to tackle just one area, like reducing your bidi expenses, to help take some pressure off her?

That's a good idea, I guess. I've been spending around 300 rupees a month on bidis, so if I could cut that back, it would help out a bit. But how would we actually do that? I don't see how just cutting back on bidis is gonna fix everything.

You're still unsure about how cutting back on bidi expenses will help, and that's a valid concern. Would considering a simple action like drinking water regularly when you feel the urge to smoke help distract you from those cravings and make it easier to stay on track?
Drinking water's not a bad idea, I suppose. But I've tried to quit before and it didn't work. I'm not sure why it would be different this time.

You're still unsure about why the previous attempts to quit didn't work and are worried that this time might be the same. Do you think it's possible that you might be approaching this quit attempt with a different mindset or attitude, even if you're not fully aware of it, that could make a difference?
I don't know... I've been thinking about it a bit, and maybe I am coming at it differently this time. But I'm not sure if that's gonna be enough, you know?

You're considering that you might be approaching this quit attempt with a different mindset, which is a good sign. If you're feeling more confident about quitting this time, would you be willing to think about a specific goal for the next few days, like going 2-3 days without smoking in social situations?
I don't know... that sounds like a pretty small goal, but I guess it's a start. What if I said I'd try to go 2 days without smoking on the construction site?

You're considering setting a goal to stay smoke-free for 2 days on the construction site, which is a great start. Would you be willing to think about what you can do in those two days to make that goal more achievable?
I can try to avoid my coworkers who always offer me a bidi at first, and see how that goes. Maybe if I can make it through those two days without smoking, it'll give me some momentum to keep going.

You're thinking about avoiding your coworkers who offer you bidis as a way to stay on track. Would you be open to having a simple phrase ready to use if they do offer you a bidi, like "I'm doing okay, thanks, I'm trying to take care of my health"?
I smoke bidis and eat paan. Started when I was around 25. Now it's maybe 7 or 8 bidis a day, down from more before.

It sounds like you've been smoking bidis and eating paan for a while, and you've noticed your smoking habit has decreased over time. Can you tell me more about what you usually do with paan and bidis - is it a daily routine, or do you do it in specific situations?
Paan is with meals, or sometimes when I'm watching TV. Bidis, I usually have when I wake up early, around 3 AM. Can't sleep, so I think about my wife, and have a bidi.

It sounds like paan and bidis are closely tied to your daily routines and emotional moments, especially when you're thinking about your wife. Can you tell me more about what's going through your mind when you think about your wife that makes you reach for a bidi?

It's hard to explain... I miss her. It's been two years now, but it still feels like yesterday that she was here. I get up in the middle of the night, and it's like her absence hits me the most. I think of all the things we'd do together, the conversations we'd have, and I feel lonely. The bidi is just something I've always done, I suppose. It's a habit, but it's also... a comfort, I guess.

It sounds like the bidi has become a coping mechanism for your loneliness and missing your wife. Can you think of something else that might provide you with comfort and distraction when you're feeling lonely and missing her, something that doesn't involve smoking?

I don't know... my daughters try to get me to do things, but I just don't feel like it. Sometimes I watch old movies, but even that doesn't seem the same. My wife and I used to watch movies together, and I think it's just easier to remember the past than to do something new.  I'm not sure I can find anything to take the place of my wife.

It sounds like remembering the past and watching old movies together with your wife brings back happy memories, but also makes you miss her more. What if you started writing down some of those happy memories with your wife, like a journal, and then added some new memories with your daughters - it might help you find a way to honor your past and also build new ones with the people who are still in your life?

Writing things down... I don't know if I'm good at that kind of thing. But I suppose it couldn't hurt to try. My daughters would like that, I think. They're always trying to get me to do things, so maybe this would be something I could do with them.

It sounds like you're open to trying journaling, and you think your daughters might enjoy it too. Would you be willing to start small, like just writing down one happy memory with your wife each day, and then see how it feels to add a new memory with one of your daughters?
That sounds manageable. I can try to do that. But what if I forget? Or what if it's hard to think of things?

You're worried about forgetting or struggling to think of memories. What if you set a small timer for 5 minutes each day to write down your memories, so you can focus on just that one task and not worry about doing it perfectly?
A timer might help. It's like when I used to try to quit smoking, having a plan and a routine was helpful. Maybe this will be too, I don't know.

You're thinking of your past attempts to quit smoking and how having a plan and a routine worked for you. Does that make you feel more hopeful that this journaling thing could actually help you manage your cravings for bidis?

I guess it does. I don't feel like I'm in control right now, and if I can find something to help me feel more in control, maybe that will make a difference. It's just...I don't want to be a burden on my daughters, and if I can stay healthy, it'll make them happy, I think.
You feel like being in control is a big issue for you right now, and you think journaling might help with that. Can you think of one small step you could take today to start feeling more in control?
I could try to wake up a little later than 3 AM, and see if I can go a little longer without a bidi. Maybe start with just 15 minutes, see if I can do that.

You're feeling a bit more empowered by considering small changes in your morning routine. Would trying to do something else for the first 15 minutes after waking up - like going for a short walk or drinking a glass of water - help distract you from the craving for a bidi?
Yeah, maybe that would work. I could try going for a short walk. But it's dark outside, so that might be hard. And it might be raining too.
You're thinking about the dark and rain being potential obstacles to your walk. Would it help to make a plan for a short walk as soon as it's light outside tomorrow, so you can try it then?
That sounds like a good idea. I can look out the window and see when it starts getting light, and then go for a walk. My daughters could even come with me, that would be nice.

You're feeling more hopeful about taking a short walk with your daughters tomorrow. Would you be willing to reward yourself with something small, like a favorite coffee or a relaxing bath, if you successfully make it through tomorrow without a bidi?
That sounds like a good idea. Something to look forward to. Maybe I'll get that coffee I've been wanting.

You're feeling more motivated with the idea of rewarding yourself if you stay bidi-free tomorrow. Would it help to write down exactly what you'll do tomorrow when the craving hits, like taking a short walk with your daughters and enjoying that coffee, so you can visualize and feel more in control of the situation?
My son is being treated here, so I thought I should see someone too. I smoke when I get angry or stressed. It's hard to explain.
It sounds like you've been coming here to support your son, and now you're facing a challenge of your own. How long have you been smoking?
About 12 years, I guess. It's just something I do, you know?

It's like smoking has become a habit for you, something you're not even thinking about most of the time. Are you feeling any pressure from your son or others to quit smoking, or is this something you've thought about doing for yourself?
My wife's been on my case about it for years, but it's not like I've ever really tried to stop. My son's visit here might've made me think about it a bit more, I suppose.

It makes sense that your wife's concerns and your son's visit might be motivating you to think about quitting. Can you imagine what it would be like if you were able to manage your anger and stress without needing to smoke?
I guess so. It's hard to picture, though. Smoking's just been there, you know? It helps calm me down.

It sounds like you're struggling to imagine a life without smoking because it's been such a big part of managing your stress and anxiety. Would you be open to trying some short walks outside when you feel stressed, to see if that can help you find some calm without reaching for a cigarette?
I don't know...I like my routine. I'm not sure taking a walk would help. What's wrong with smoking, anyway? It's not like it's hurting anyone else.

You seem uncertain about changing your routine and are questioning the need to quit smoking. Can you think of one specific health benefit of quitting smoking that might resonate with you, such as living longer for your son?
..my son's not that old yet. I still have time, right? But yeah, maybe living to see him grow up, be successful...that's something to think about, I guess.

You're starting to see the importance of living a healthy life for your son's sake. Can you imagine having the energy and health to be involved in his life, attend school events, and watch him accomplish his goals?
Yeah...that sounds good. I don't want to be too tired or weak for him. But what if I mess up and start smoking again? I don't want to go back to that.

You're worried about relapsing and going back to smoking. Can you think of a simple phrase you can say to yourself when you feel the urge to smoke, like "not today" or "I can do this," to give yourself a moment to pause and reconsider?
I guess "not today" is easy enough. But what if it's every day, then?

You're worried that "not today" might not be enough to help you in the long run. Can you imagine creating a daily plan to tackle your morning cravings, such as going for a short walk or drinking a glass of water as soon as you wake up?

Short walks, yeah...that might work. And water, that's good too...I drink too little water anyway. So, like, a walk, a glass of water, and then "not today" whenever I feel like smoking? That's doable, right?

That sounds like a great plan to start with. Would you be willing to try saying the phrase "not today" out loud to yourself the first time you feel the urge to smoke, so it becomes a more intentional and powerful reminder to resist the craving?
Yeah...I can try that. It sounds kind of silly, but if it helps, I'll do it. "Not today" out loud, okay.

You're feeling more open to trying out "not today" out loud, that's a great step. Can you think of one situation where you might need to say "no" to a cigarette, like at a social gathering or when a coworker offers you one, and how you can politely decline while remembering your quit goal?
I get offered cigarettes at school when I'm on break. I can just say, "No thanks, I'm trying to quit" or something like that.
You've taken a big step today, and I'm confident you'll find a way to stay on track with your goals.

I guess I feel a little more hopeful now. Maybe I can do this. Thank you. 

END
You're feeling more hopeful about quitting, that's a huge step. Can you imagine what your life might look like a month from now, if you were successful in quitting cigarettes?
Honestly, I don't know why I'm here. I don't think smoking is a big deal. I've been doing it for 25 years and I'm fine.

It sounds like you're feeling a bit skeptical about quitting smoking, like it's not a priority for you right now. What do you think might change your mind about quitting, or make you consider it a bigger deal?
My wife's been on my case about it nonstop, but I don't think she gets it. Maybe if I had a health scare or something, but like I said, I feel fine. What's the worst that could happen, right?

It sounds like your wife's constant reminders about your smoking are a bit frustrating for you, and you're not really considering quitting right now because you feel physically fine. What role do you think your wife plays in your daily smoking habit - does she smoke too, or is she often around when you light up?
No, my wife doesn't smoke. She's always telling me to quit, though. I guess it makes her uncomfortable to be around when I'm smoking. But it's my shop, I can do what I want in it, right?
It sounds like you feel like your shop is your private space and that quitting smoking is your decision to make. What do you usually do when your wife comes into the shop and you're smoking?
I usually hide it a bit, or step outside for a quick one. I don't want her to see me smoking, but it's hard to resist when I'm stuck in the shop for hours. I mean, it's my break, right?

It sounds like hiding or stepping outside to smoke is a common pattern for you when your wife comes into the shop. Can you imagine taking a step further and actually telling your wife you're going outside for a break, just to get away from her, rather than to smoke?

I guess so. I mean, it's possible I could do that. But what's the point? She'd just find something else to complain about. Maybe I'd just tell her I'm going to get some fresh air or something, so she doesn't think I'm sneaking off to smoke.

It sounds like you're feeling a bit trapped in the situation with your wife and the shop. Can you think of a way to approach her about your smoking in a way that feels more like you're in control of the situation, rather than sneaking around?

I don't know... maybe I could just tell her I know she's worried about me, and that I appreciate it, but I need to be honest with her - I'm not ready to quit yet. See how she reacts. Might make her back off.

That takes a lot of courage to consider having an honest conversation with your wife about your smoking. What do you think would be the most challenging part of being honest with her about your not being ready to quit yet?

I think it's just the guilt thing. She's always telling me it's bad for my health, and I don't want to hear it anymore. But at the same time, I don't want to feel like I'm lying to her when I say I'm not quitting. Maybe I just need to be prepared for how she'll react, you know?

It sounds like you're feeling a mix of guilt and fear about being honest with your wife. Have you ever written down exactly why quitting smoking is important to you, and how it would benefit both you and your family?

No, I haven't really thought that much about it, to be honest. I mean, I guess it would be nice to be around for my wife and kids longer, but it's hard to think about when I'm perfectly fine right now. Writing it down might be a good idea, though. Maybe it would help me see things from a different perspective.

You're considering writing down why quitting smoking is important to you, which could help give you a clearer sense of your motivations. Would you be willing to try a simple exercise where you write down the specific benefits of quitting smoking, such as being around for family events or having more energy, and seeing how it affects your perspective?
Yeah, okay. That doesn't sound too bad. I'll try it. But can I do it here, or do I need to take it home?
You're taking that first step, and I have no doubt you'll make great progress – keep going, you're doing great.
Thanks, I guess I am doing a bit better than I thought. Yeah, I'll write it down here.
Now that you're feeling more motivated, what specific benefits of quitting smoking do you think you'll write down first, to get started with this exercise?

I think I'll start with being able to breathe better, and not getting winded as easily. That's something I've noticed when I've been away from the shop for a while, but then I start smoking again and I get tired more easily. Maybe if I can get that sorted out, it'll be easier to deal with the rest.

You've identified a specific benefit of quitting smoking that's really meaningful to you, and that's a great starting point. Now, can you imagine what it would be like to have that benefit every day, instead of just when you're not smoking?
Yeah... I think it would be pretty nice. I could maybe even play with my grandkids without getting winded, that'd be great. My wife would like that too, she's always asking me to play with them more.

That's a lovely image of what a healthier life could look like for you and your loved ones. Would you be open to keeping your hands busy with some activity when you feel the urge to smoke, like drawing or using a stress ball?
I've been smoking for 20 years and I've tried to quit many times. I don't know why I'm even here. It never works out.

I can see how frustrated and defeated you must feel after all those attempts. Can you think of anything that made you feel like you were doing okay in your previous quit attempts, even if it was just for a short time?
I did okay with the nicotine gum once. I made it a week without smoking, but then I got so anxious and started eating more, and I just couldn't take it anymore.

You made it a whole week without smoking that time, that's a big accomplishment. What if we tried a different approach with the nicotine gum this time, like chewing it slowly and then placing it between your cheek and gum to help manage your anxiety?

That sounds like it might help, but it's not just the anxiety... it's the weight gain I'm really scared of. I've tried to quit so many times and I've always gained weight, and it's hard to deal with that on top of the withdrawal.

I understand your concerns about weight gain, that can be a really tough challenge when quitting smoking. Have you ever noticed any differences in how you eat when you're not smoking, like are you more likely to grab quick, high-calorie snacks or do you tend to reach for healthier options?

I think I eat more comfort foods when I'm stressed or bored, which is usually when I smoke. And if I'm not smoking, I just end up eating more because I'm hungry, but I'm also snacking out of habit or boredom. It's like my mind needs something to do.

You've noticed that when you're feeling stressed or bored, you tend to reach for comfort foods, and that's also when you usually smoke. Have you ever tried doing something physical, like taking a short walk, when you're feeling that way, just to see if it helps distract you from both the hunger and the urge to smoke?

I've walked a lot, I live close to the park and I love taking walks. But it's not like it stops me from wanting to smoke afterwards. It's like my craving is still there, even if I've just walked for an hour.

You've had some success with walking, but the craving still persists afterwards. Can you think of a way to break up your walks into smaller, more manageable chunks, maybe taking a shorter walk every hour or so to see if that helps you stay focused on the physical activity and not get caught up in the craving afterwards?
That might be worth trying. I could take a 10-minute walk every hour or so, that's not so overwhelming. But what if I'm at home and I'm bored? What can I do then?

You're thinking about how to stay active and focused when you're at home, and boredom is a big trigger for you. What if we tried delaying your response to a craving by just 10 minutes, so you can take a few deep breaths and do something else, like folding laundry or making a quick phone call, before deciding what to do?
That sounds like something I could try. But what if the craving is really bad, like after a meal? I've always thought about how hard it would be to quit right after eating.

You're feeling that changing your environment might be particularly challenging after eating, like you're more likely to stay home and sit down. Would you be willing to try moving to a different room in the house, like the living room or the backyard, right after a meal, just to get some distance from the triggers that might be making you want to smoke?
That might work, but I'd still have to deal with the urge, and if I'm not smoking, I'm afraid I'll just eat more or do something else unhealthy.

You're worried that quitting smoking will lead to overeating or other unhealthy behaviors. How do you think we could prepare for those kinds of situations, so you feel more in control and confident in your choices?

I guess if I was eating healthier, like fruits and veggies, instead of junk food, I might feel better about myself and not need to smoke or overeat. But how do I do that? I don't know much about healthy eating.

You're considering how healthy eating might help you manage your cravings and boost your self-esteem. Would you be open to trying to incorporate some small, easy-to-eat healthy snacks, like fruits or carrot sticks, into your day, especially after meals, to see if it can help distract you from the urge to smoke?
That sounds doable. I could try keeping some fruit or carrot sticks in the car or at my desk, so I have them when I need them. But what if it doesn't work? What if I still want to smoke?

You're feeling a bit uncertain about whether this new strategy will work, and that's totally normal. What if instead of focusing on whether it "works", we focus on how it makes you feel, even if it's just a small sense of accomplishment or a tiny distraction from the craving?

I guess I just want to feel like I'm making progress, even if it's small. And if I can feel good about myself for trying to eat healthier, that might help me feel more confident about quitting smoking.

You're really connecting the dots between eating healthier and feeling more confident in your choices. What if we made a specific plan for what healthy snacks you'll have available to you at home and in the car, so you can have a stash of them ready to go when cravings hit, especially in situations like after meals or when you're feeling bored?

That sounds like a good idea. I could try keeping some of those healthy snacks in a container or bag, so it's easy to grab something when I need it. And maybe having a few of those snacks ready to go in the car, like some nuts or dried fruit, would help me if I'm out and about and feel like smoking.

You're thinking ahead and wanting to be prepared with healthy snacks in the car - that's a great step. What do you think about trying to have a specific routine, like opening the container with healthy snacks every time you get into the car, to make it a new habit?
Look, I work at a dhaba and everyone smokes here. It's part of the culture. I don't really want to quit, but the doctor insisted I come.
It sounds like working at the dhaba makes it really challenging for you to not smoke. How many cigarettes do you typically smoke per day?
About 12, maybe a few bidis thrown in here and there. It's just a habit, you know?
So it sounds like you view smoking as a habit, not really a choice. What do you think about when you're not at the dhaba - do you still think about smoking?
Yeah, I guess I do. When I'm hanging out with friends at the tea stall, or after a tough day of work, I'll pick up a cigarette. It's just something I do, I guess.
You mention that after a tough day of work, you pick up a cigarette. If you were to quit, what would be the biggest benefit for you that would make it worth giving up that cigarette?
Honestly, not much comes to mind. My friends would probably give me a hard time, think I'm being a goody-goody.

It sounds like you're worried about what others might think of you quitting smoking. Can you imagine having someone close to you who genuinely supports and believes in your ability to quit, and would be there to encourage you on tough days?
My younger brother has been trying to get me to quit for years, maybe he'd be more supportive if I tried. I don't know, he's got a lot on his plate with work and all, but I guess it's worth a shot.
It sounds like you're considering reaching out to your younger brother for support. What do you think would be the first step in having a conversation with him about quitting smoking?
Maybe just talking to him about how it's affecting my health, see how he reacts. I don't know, I don't want to put more pressure on him or make him feel like he's got to help me out or anything.

It sounds like you're considering how to approach your brother without putting any pressure on him. Can you imagine what you would say to him if you were to have this conversation, and how you think he might respond?

Yeah, I can imagine it. I'd just tell him what the doctor said, and how I'm feeling, and see how he reacts. He'll probably just tell me to stop smoking again, but if he's listening, maybe he'll actually say something useful this time.

It sounds like you're thinking about how to approach your brother in a way that feels natural and non-pushy. What do you think would be your response if someone, like a friend or coworker, offered you a cigarette in a social situation, like at a party or while taking a break at work?

I'd probably just say no, or change the subject. It wouldn't be easy, but I don't know, maybe if I've been doing this for a while, I'd be brave enough to just say no and not feel like I'm missing out or anything.
You've thought about how to respond to a social situation. That's a big step. Can you imagine yourself saying "no" to a cigarette and feeling confident and proud of your decision?
Yeah, maybe I can. I mean, it's not like I've never done anything hard in my life. I just moved to the city a few years ago, that was tough. Quitting smoking can't be that different, right?
You're starting to see that you've overcome tough challenges before, like moving to the city. How did you prepare for that move, and what skills or strengths did you use to get through it?

Uh, I just kind of winged it, to be honest. I mean, I saved up some money, found a place to stay, and started looking for a job. I guess I just used my common sense and stuff. It wasn't that hard, but at the time, it felt like a big deal.

It sounds like you were able to use your practical skills to navigate a big change in your life. Can you think of how you might apply some of that common sense to preparing for this quit attempt, to make it feel more manageable and less overwhelming?

I guess I could, yeah. Start by setting small goals, like not smoking during work hours or something. And maybe find some ways to cope with stress, like exercise or meditation or something. That might help.

It sounds like you're already thinking about breaking down the quit attempt into smaller, manageable steps, which is a great start. Are you willing to try writing down these small goals and keeping them somewhere visible to help stay on track?
Yeah, sure. I could do that. I've got a notebook and pen in my room, I'll just jot down some stuff there. Nothing too formal, just a reminder to myself.
You've already decided to start writing down your small goals to stay on track. What would be the first thing you'd like to write down as one of those goals?
I quit for about 3 months last year. Then a really bad week at work happened and I started again. Now I'm back to 15 a day.
It takes a lot of courage to try quitting and then go back to it, especially when life got really tough at work. What did you learn from that experience that might help you this time around?

I guess the only thing I can think of is that I got too caught up in trying to solve all my problems at once and forgot about quitting. I just needed something to take my mind off things, and smoking was there.

It sounds like you felt overwhelmed and turned to smoking as a way to cope with your stress, and then got caught up in trying to solve everything at once. Can you think of a healthier alternative that you could turn to when you're feeling overwhelmed, like a short walk or a hobby?

Yeah, I guess I used to run a lot, but stopped when I got really busy with work. Maybe I could start doing that again, or even just going for a short walk around the block. What do you think about that?

That's great to hear you're thinking of getting back into running or walking. Have you considered starting with an even smaller, more manageable goal, like a 5-10 minute walk each day, to build back into your routine?
I don't know, maybe that's too simple. I've been so used to thinking, "Okay, I'm going for a 30-minute run," that starting with something smaller feels like cheating.

It sounds like you're worried that starting small feels like selling yourself short, but what if I told you that even if you can only walk for 5 minutes a day, that's still a step in the right direction. Can you imagine what it would feel like to be able to walk for 5 minutes without feeling the urge to smoke afterwards?

Yeah... actually, that's a pretty good feeling. It's kind of like what I felt when I quit for 3 months the first time. But I don't know... it just feels like it's too easy, you know? I'm an IT guy, not some yoga teacher who's always talking about mindfulness and stuff.

It sounds like you're feeling a bit skeptical about the idea of starting small, and you're worried that it's too simple or not "tough" enough. Can you explore with me what you think it would take to quit smoking, and whether that's something that's even possible for you?

I guess what I'm thinking is that quitting smoking is like trying to fix everything that's wrong with the world - you can't just do it, it's too big. But at the same time, I really want to quit, and I'm willing to try. I just don't know if I'm willing to do it in small steps.

You're feeling overwhelmed by the idea of quitting smoking because it feels like such a big task. Can you imagine having someone to hold you accountable and support you through these small steps, like a friend or family member who's also quitting?

Actually, my wife has been on my case about quitting for a while now. She's pretty good about not nagging me, but I can tell she's really worried about my health. Maybe I could quit for her, as much as for myself.

That's a great insight about considering quitting for your wife, and also for yourself. What do you think would be the most challenging part of explaining to your wife that you're quitting for her, rather than just for your own reasons?
I don't know, it just feels like I'm being dishonest if I say it's for her when it's really for me too. I guess I'd be worried about her not wanting me to quit out of some sort of pity or something.

Let's explore this idea of honesty and how it affects your motivation. Can you imagine what would happen if you were to share your true reasons for quitting with your wife, including both your personal concerns and your desire to quit for her as well?
That might actually feel kind of liberating, you know? Like, I'm not just trying to fix everything for myself, I'm also doing it because I care about someone else. Maybe that makes it more real.

You're feeling a sense of freedom in considering your true reasons for quitting. Would you be willing to start exploring those reasons in more detail, perhaps by writing them down in a journal or talking about them with your wife?
Yeah, that sounds okay, I guess. I'm not one of those guys who writes in a journal or anything, but if it'll help... do you think it would be better to do it with my wife or by myself?

You're open to trying something new. Before we decide on the journaling part, would you be willing to start small and just write down one thing you value about your wife that makes you want to quit smoking for her?
I think that's a pretty manageable task. One thing that comes to mind is that she's always looking out for my health, even when I'm not looking out for it myself.

Now that you've identified one thing that motivates you to quit, can you imagine how it feels to have your wife's support and encouragement, and how that might impact your ability to resist cravings when you're feeling stressed or bored at work?
It's weird, but just thinking about it makes me feel a little more motivated to get through this tough stuff. Like, knowing she's got my back makes it feel less overwhelming or something.

You're feeling more motivated now. Would you be willing to start writing down some of those moments when you feel supported by your wife, so we can look for patterns and see if we can apply that to resisting cravings when you're feeling stressed or bored at work?
I smoke because it calms me down when I feel anxious. I know it makes things worse but I can't stop.
I can see that anxiety plays a big role in your smoking habit. How many cigarettes do you typically smoke in a day?
Around 8-10, but some days it's more if I'm working on a big project or in a really crowded space. It helps me breathe, I guess.
You're saying that smoking helps you breathe, especially in crowded spaces. What kind of work do you do that involves working on big projects?
I'm a freelance designer, so I work on my own projects, and sometimes those deadlines can be super overwhelming. And yeah, crowded spaces, like events or parties, make me feel really anxious too.
It sounds like tight deadlines and crowded spaces really impact your anxiety levels. How long have you been smoking, if you don't mind me asking?
It's hard to think about, but... 5 years, I guess. It's just become a part of my life, but I don't really see it as a big deal, I mean, everyone I hang out with smokes too. It's like, normal.
It sounds like you've been smoking for so long, it feels like a normal part of your life. How do you think your life would change if you weren't smoking, like in a year from now?

I... don't know. I guess I would feel more in control, not having to rely on cigarettes to calm down. But what if I'm not calm? What if I'm still anxious? It's scary to think about quitting, because I don't know if I can handle that.
You're worried that you won't know how to calm down without cigarettes. Can you imagine what you might do instead of smoking when you're feeling anxious in the future?

..  I guess I could try deep breathing, like my grandma taught me when I was a kid. Or, uh, take a short walk outside. But it's hard to imagine, you know? It's not like I've ever actually tried quitting or done anything that doesn't involve smoking.

You've mentioned deep breathing and taking a short walk outside as things you've tried before, and they might be helpful in certain situations. What if we practiced having a "quit plan" for specific situations like crowded spaces, so you feel more prepared to handle them without smoking?

That sounds kind of... helpful, I guess. But it's not like I can just stop smoking tomorrow. I mean, what if I'm at a party and it's super crowded, and I just feel like I need a cigarette? I don't know if I can handle that.

It sounds like you're worried about not having a plan for dealing with cravings in situations like crowded parties. Can you imagine how it would feel to be able to politely say no to a cigarette offered to you in a situation like that, and to remember why you decided to quit in the first place?

It feels... good to think about, I guess. I mean, it's nice to imagine being able to say no and feeling proud of myself for quitting. But it's hard to picture actually being in that situation and feeling proud of myself. It feels more like, I don't know... I'd probably just end up smoking again anyway.

You're starting to imagine having more control over situations that trigger your desire to smoke, like crowded parties. What if you wrote down specific reasons why quitting is important to you, like the reasons you mentioned earlier, family pressure, weight concerns, and feeling in control, to remind yourself of your motivations when you're in those situations?

.. I don't know if that would really help. I mean, it's not like I forget why I want to quit. It's just... it feels hard to actually do it, you know? But if it would help, I could try writing them down, I guess.

It sounds like you're feeling uncertain about whether writing down your reasons for quitting will actually make a difference. Can you think of a specific time when you were able to resist the urge to smoke for a short period of time, even if it was just for a day or two?

I think so... there was this one time when I was on a plane and I forgot my cigarettes in my other bag, and I didn't have any cash to buy more at the airport. I was really anxious, but I just didn't have a cigarette, so I... I got through it, I guess. It was weird. I didn't even miss it that much.

You're recalling a time when you were able to resist smoking for a while without even missing it that much. What was going through your mind during those moments when you felt anxious but didn't give in to the urge to smoke?

.. I don't know, it just didn't feel as bad, I guess. I was more focused on getting to my destination and dealing with the flight itself. I didn't have time to worry about being anxious or needing a cigarette. It was like, I was problem-solving or something, instead of smoking.

It sounds like being engaged in a different activity, problem-solving, helped take your mind off the urge to smoke. Can you think about how you might apply that same kind of focus to handling anxiety in other situations, like crowded spaces or overwhelming deadlines?

.. yeah, it's possible. I mean, I've done that before when I'm working on a project and completely absorbed in it. Maybe I just need to find more things like that to focus on when I'm feeling anxious. But it's hard to think about doing that in, like, a party or something.

You're acknowledging that finding alternative focus can be helpful in managing your anxiety. Can you think of one specific activity you could do at a party to shift your focus away from the urge to smoke?
I had quit for 2 full years. Then I went to a party, had a few drinks, and one of my friends offered a cigarette. That was 6 months ago. Now I'm back to 10-15 a day.

That's a tough relapse to go through, especially after making it 2 years without smoking. What was it about being at the party, having a few drinks, and having a friend offer you a cigarette that made you feel like smoking again?
I guess it just felt like the old me, you know? We were all laughing, having a good time, and it was like nothing had changed, even though I knew I'd relapsed.

It sounds like being at the party and surrounded by people who were smoking and laughing triggered a sense of nostalgia and feeling like you fit in. What was going through your mind when your friend offered you a cigarette - was it a moment of temptation, or did you feel like you were being pulled back into a familiar routine?

It was more like a default, I guess. I didn't really think about whether I wanted to smoke or not, it was just something I did in those situations. My friend didn't even ask if I wanted one, he just assumed I'd be smoking and handed me one.

It sounds like your friend's assumption that you'd be smoking was a big part of what made you feel like you were slipping back into your old routine. What was the moment when you realized you had taken a cigarette, and did you feel any regret or hesitation after you'd already started smoking?

Yeah, I think it was after I had my first puff that I realized what was happening. But by then, it was too late, I'd already lit it up. There was a bit of regret, but it was also...I don't know, it felt like I was just going along with what everyone else was doing, you know? It wasn't like I was thinking clearly about it.

It sounds like you felt a strong sense of social pressure and a desire to fit in, which led you to act impulsively. Have you ever thought about practicing a simple response to decline a cigarette in advance, so you're better prepared for situations like the party?

Honestly, it never occurred to me to think about it that way. I mean, I've quit before, so I knew I had the power to say no, but I guess I just wasn't thinking ahead. Yeah, having a response ready would be a good idea, I can see how that would make a difference.

You've got a good understanding of how having a response ready can help in situations like the party. Can you imagine what a simple, polite way to decline a cigarette might look like, and what you think would make it feel more natural for you to say no?

Like, I don't know, maybe something simple like "Thanks, but I'm good" or "I'm trying to cut back". I think if I had a few of those phrases in my head ahead of time, it would be easier to say no when someone offered me a cigarette. And maybe if I could figure out why I feel like I need to smoke in social situations, I could address that root cause too.

You're already thinking about ways to politely decline a cigarette, and that's a great start. Can you imagine using a phrase like "Thanks, but I'm good" in a situation where a friend offers you a cigarette while you're at a party?

Yeah, I think I could do it. It's not like it's a weird or awkward thing to say, right? But at the same time, I'm not sure I can guarantee that I would say it, especially if it's a close friend who's offering it to me.

You're thinking that even having a polite response ready might not be enough to guarantee saying no to a close friend. How do you feel when a close friend offers you a cigarette, is it like they're trying to help or is it more about not wanting to let them down?

It feels like I'm letting them down, I guess. Like, it's not just about the cigarette, it's about us hanging out and having a good time together, and if I'm not smoking, it feels like I'm missing out on some part of that experience.

You feel like you're letting your friends down by not smoking, and that it's tied to feeling like you're missing out on a part of the experience. Can you think of one person in your life who you feel would be supportive and understanding if you told them you're trying to quit smoking?

Actually, my sister comes to mind. She's always been really supportive of me, and she's never pressured me to do anything I don't want to do. I think if I told her I'm trying to quit smoking, she'd be really understanding and might even offer to help me out.

It sounds like your sister could be a great source of support for you in your quitting journey. Have you thought about talking to her about your decision to quit and asking her if she'd be willing to be that person for you, someone you can call or text when you're feeling the urge to smoke?
Yeah, that's a good idea. I can see having her as a backup plan, someone I can reach out to when I'm feeling tempted. Just talking to her about it might even make me more committed to quitting.
It sounds like you're feeling more confident and empowered about quitting, and I wish you all the best in this new chapter.

You're feeling more confident about quitting and even thinking of talking to your sister for support. Would you be open to trying a strategy where you reward yourself for small milestones, like staying smoke-free for a day or a week, to help motivate you during tough moments?
I've tried to quit 3 times. The longest I went was 3 months. But I just can't seem to stay quit. My daughter is growing up and I don't want her to see me like this.

It sounds like you're really motivated to quit for your daughter's sake, and that's a beautiful reason to want to change. What did you do during those three months when you were able to stop smoking, and what made it harder for you to stay quit after that?

To be honest, it was just a lot of willpower and distraction at first. I'd go for walks, join a gym, and just stay busy. But then work started getting stressful again and...  it felt like my lungs would burn if I didn't have a cigarette. And I'd get so anxious during meetings, that's when the cravings hit the hardest.
It sounds like you've had some success with staying busy and exercising to distract yourself from cravings. Have you considered taking a short walk during those stressful meetings at work?

That's not always possible, especially if I'm in a meeting or have a lot on my plate. And even if I do go for a walk, I usually end up feeling stressed again by the time I get back. Plus, driving home afterwards is the worst. I get anxious and the traffic just makes me want to smoke even more.

You're saying that taking a walk doesn't always work for you when you're in a meeting or have a lot to do, and it even sometimes makes your anxiety worse. Have you ever tried doing something with your hands during those moments, like doodling or playing with a stress ball, to see if that distracts you from the craving?

I might have tried doodling once or twice, but I'm not really artistic and it just feels silly drawing during a meeting. A stress ball sounds interesting, though. I don't know, if it's not something I see people using, I'm not sure how effective it would be.

You're not sure if doodling feels silly in a meeting, but a stress ball caught your attention. Would you be willing to try a small, discreet stress ball, like a small rubber wristband, to see if it might help calm your hands and distract you from cravings?

Yeah, maybe. A rubber wristband doesn't sound too embarrassing. I could try that, but what if it doesn't work? And what about when I'm driving? I don't want to be fidgeting with a stress ball and taking my eyes off the road.

You're considering trying the rubber wristband but have concerns about its effectiveness and using it while driving. What if we adapted the stress ball idea to something even more discreet, like a small, portable fidget toy that you could easily hold in your hand while driving?

That sounds a bit better. I mean, I've seen those little fidget toys online that people use to calm down in meetings or classes. I could try one of those, maybe it would help. But what if it's just something else to keep in my bag, take up space, and remind me of how hard it is to quit?

You're worried that the fidget toy might just add to the clutter in your bag and be a constant reminder of your struggles with quitting. Are you open to the idea of keeping the fidget toy in a designated, small pouch or container, so it's not just floating around in your bag?
I guess that's a good idea. If it's in a separate pouch, it's not going to be taking up space in my bag and it's not going to be staring at me all the time. That might actually be kind of helpful.

It sounds like you're feeling a little more hopeful about trying a discreet fidget toy to help manage your cravings. What specific situations or triggers do you think you might use the fidget toy to help calm yourself in, like during morning cravings or work stress?

I think the driving thing would be tough, but during meetings and when I'm getting anxious about work, it might actually help. And mornings, yeah, mornings are usually when I crave a cigarette the most, so maybe that's something I could try using the fidget toy for.

You're considering using the fidget toy during mornings when you usually crave a cigarette. Do you think using it as soon as you wake up, like brushing your teeth or drinking water, might be a good way to break the chain of reaching for a cigarette first thing in the morning?

Yeah, using it right away makes sense. If I'm already holding onto the fidget toy, it might make me think twice about reaching for a cigarette. And it's something I can do automatically, like brushing my teeth, so maybe that would actually work.
I'm glad you're feeling more hopeful about breaking the smoking habit – take care of yourself and keep moving forward.

You're considering using the fidget toy right away in the morning, which sounds like a good plan. What do you think about using a small reward, like a nice cup of coffee or a short break, after you've made it through your morning without smoking, to reinforce this new habit?
I chew gutkha, not cigarettes. About 8 to 10 pouches a day for the last 7 years. My mouth has developed some kind of white patch and the doctor is worried.
I can see how concerned you are about your health, especially with that white patch in your mouth. When you're feeling stressed or anxious, do you find yourself reaching for gutkha more often?
Long driving shifts, red light stops... yeah, those times I use it to pass the time. Sometimes when I'm bored between trips too. It's a habit, I guess.

It sounds like you use gutkha as a way to occupy yourself during long drives and at red light stops. Have you ever tried taking a short walk or doing some light stretching during those breaks instead of reaching for gutkha?

It's hard to walk around when I'm waiting for passengers at red lights. People might just not notice me or get angry if I'm blocking the road. Plus, how long can you really walk around in a minute or two?

I understand that you feel like taking a walk isn't feasible at red light stops due to the time constraint and potential inconvenience to others. Can you think of any other small action you could do during those short breaks that might help distract you from the urge to chew gutkha?
I could... listen to music or maybe podcasts? I've got a phone with internet, but like, what would I do on my breaks?
You're considering listening to music or podcasts during your breaks, and that's a great idea. Would you be open to trying that and seeing if it can help distract you from the urge to chew gutkha?
Yeah, okay. I'll try it. But how do I make sure I don't just start thinking about chewing gutkha while listening to music?
It sounds like you're feeling more hopeful and empowered to try new strategies.
Maybe this will actually work. What do I do with my hands when I'm waiting for passengers, though? I always end up reaching for my pouch.

It sounds like you're really thinking about this and wanting to make it work. Can you try keeping a small notebook or a pen with you so you can doodle or write down a to-do list when you're waiting for passengers?
I never thought of that. That way, my hands won't just be idle, right? But what about in the evenings, after dinner? That's when I usually have a pouch with me...

You're thinking ahead and considering how to stay occupied in the evenings after dinner. Can you try getting up and doing something light, like brushing your teeth or taking a short walk, right after dinner to break the habit of reaching for your pouch?
That sounds okay, I guess. I should try to break the habit before it starts. But what if I still want something to do with my hands after dinner?
You're thinking about how to keep your hands occupied after dinner. Can you try chewing sugar-free gum right after dinner, just to see how it feels and if it helps distract you from your cravings?

I've tried that sugar-free gum thing before, but it didn't really help me back then. This time, I'll try it and see. Do you think it might work if I make a deal with myself to chew it for a certain amount of time, like 20 minutes?
It sounds like you're willing to give sugar-free gum another try. Can you imagine how it might feel to have your hands occupied and a clean mouth after chewing it for 20 minutes?
Yeah... it does feel good to think about that. Maybe this is a start, maybe I can do this. I'm gonna try it.
You're taking a courageous step forward – I wish you all the support and success as you move forward with this new approach.

You're willing to give sugar-free gum another try. What would be the ideal time for you to start chewing it after dinner, right when you're sitting down to eat or a bit later, after you've finished your meal?
I started smoking 2 years ago in college. My friends all do it. I'm on the cricket team and my stamina is getting really bad now.

It sounds like smoking became a part of your college experience with friends, and now it's affecting your cricket performance and stamina. What was it about college that made you feel like you needed to fit in with your friends by smoking?
We'd hang out after classes, and it was just something they all did. I didn't want to stand out, you know, be the odd one out. I'm only doing it to stay part of the group, I think.

You're saying that you started smoking to fit in with your friends, so you wouldn't feel like you stood out. What kinds of situations now, besides just hanging out after classes, make you feel like you need to have a cigarette to fit in?

Like before exams, they'd all be smoking and I'd join in. I didn't think it would affect my studies, but maybe it does. And also when we're all watching a big match, they'd light up and I'd feel left out if I didn't.

You're saying that smoking was a way for you to fit in with your friends during stressful situations like exams and big matches. What if you were to have a small notebook and pen with you during those times, just to keep your hands busy - would you be willing to try that?
That's a weird idea, but I suppose it could work. But how would that stop the urge to smoke, exactly? I'm still gonna be around my friends who are smoking.

I understand your concerns about trying something new when you're still around your friends who smoke. If you were to have a small notebook and pen with you during those times, and it helped distract you from smoking, you might be able to focus more on the conversations and activities with your friends rather than feeling left out. Would that make it easier for you to imagine trying it?
I guess it's worth a shot. But what if it doesn't work? I don't want to look like a total fail in front of my friends.

You're worried about what your friends might think if you try something new and it doesn't work out. Does that make you think about how your friends might actually be more supportive than you're giving them credit for?

I don't know...Maybe they would be okay if I told them I'm trying to quit. But what if they're not? They're always joking about it, asking me to light up with them...I don't know if they'd really understand.

You're feeling unsure about whether your friends would be supportive if you told them you're trying to quit. Have you ever had a conversation with them about how smoking affects you, or is it something you've just kept to yourself?
No, never. I don't think I could face it, you know? They'd think I'm weak or something.

You're feeling really vulnerable about sharing your concerns with your friends. Have you ever noticed what happens when you do share something personal with them, like a joke or a problem, how do they usually respond?
Yeah...Actually, they're pretty good about it. They listen and make fun of me too, but it's all in a good way. They don't judge me or anything. I guess that's why I feel safe around them...

It sounds like you feel comfortable sharing personal things with your friends, and they respond in a supportive way. Would you be open to talking to one of them about your decision to quit smoking, and maybe even asking them for their support?
Yeah, I can try that. I think about it, Alex is a close friend...If I told him, he'd probably understand. I'll think about it, yeah.

You're considering talking to Alex about your decision to quit smoking, and you think he might be supportive. Will you think about having a specific conversation with Alex about your quit plan, and what you might say to him to ask for his support?
Yeah, I'll think about it. I'll talk to Alex when we're hanging out and see how it goes, I guess.

You're open to talking to Alex about your decision to quit smoking, which could be a big help. Would you be willing to think about preparing a simple message to share with Alex, like "Hey, I'm trying to quit smoking and I could use your support"?
I can try. That sounds easy enough, yeah. "Hey, I'm trying to quit smoking and I could use your support"...I can do that.

You're feeling more confident about reaching out to Alex for support, and you've even thought of a simple way to ask for his help. What do you think might happen if Alex says yes to supporting you in quitting smoking?
If Alex says yes, it would mean a lot to me. I think I might actually be able to do it, you know? Quit smoking, I mean. It would be a big weight off my shoulders.

You're feeling hopeful about having Alex's support, and it sounds like it could make a big difference in your ability to quit smoking. When you're in social situations, like when you hang out with Alex, how do you think you'll handle it if someone offers you a cigarette?
My son brought me here. I smoke 4-5 bidis a day and eat paan. I've been doing this for 30 years. I don't think I can stop at this age.

It sounds like your son's concern is what brought you here, and you're worried about quitting at this stage in your life. What do you usually do in the morning after waking up, is smoking part of your daily routine then?
No, I wake up, have chai, then start my daily chores. I don't usually smoke in the mornings. I smoke more in the afternoons when I rest for a bit after cooking.
It sounds like you're someone who values routine and relaxation time, and that's when you tend to smoke more. What's going through your mind when you feel the urge to smoke in the afternoons?
I just feel like I need it, you know? It's like my mind tells me it's time to take a break and I reach for a bidi. It's nothing to do with hunger or anything, just this feeling that I need a smoke.

It sounds like you're describing a strong emotional connection to smoking, like it's become a habit to turn to for relaxation. What do you think would happen in your life if you were able to break this pattern and find other ways to manage your downtime?

I don't know, I just feel like it's who I am. My grandmother used to smoke all the time, and now I do. It's just what we do, you know? I'm not sure I could be without it, but my son keeps saying I'm doing it for my health...

It sounds like you're feeling uncertain about who you are without smoking, and that's a really tough thing to face. Do you think your grandmother ever wanted to quit smoking, or was it just something she did as part of her daily routine as well?
My grandmother just did it, it was a habit for her too. I don't think she ever thought about stopping. I'm not sure she wanted to, it was just a part of her life.

It sounds like your grandmother's smoking habits were deeply ingrained in her daily routine, almost like a part of who she was. Can you imagine what it might be like to try breaking this cycle of behavior, not for someone else, but for yourself?
I suppose so... my son says I can do it, that I'm strong enough. But what's the point at my age? I've been smoking for 30 years...

You're feeling a bit skeptical about quitting at this stage in your life, and it's natural to wonder if it's worth the effort. Would you be willing to try rewarding yourself for small milestones, like a day or a week without smoking, to see if it gives you a sense of accomplishment and motivation to keep going?
Maybe. I like sweets, and my granddaughter makes the best gulab jamun. If I can go a week without smoking, she might make me some. That would be nice.

It sounds like the promise of a special treat from your granddaughter is motivating you to consider a week without smoking. Would you be willing to try walking outside each morning to get some fresh air and sunlight, which might help reduce your morning cravings?
I like walking outside, it's good for my garden. But what if I get cravings and can't resist smoking? I don't want to fail my granddaughter...

You're worried about not being able to resist cravings and feeling like you'd be a failure to your granddaughter if you slip up. Can you imagine what you would say to your granddaughter if she struggled with something she wanted to achieve, but was worried about making a mistake?
I would tell her it's okay, that mistakes happen, and it's how we learn. I would say, "Beta, try again, one step at a time." Maybe I can do the same for myself.

It sounds like you're starting to realize that it's okay to slip up and that you can learn from those mistakes. Would you be willing to try writing down your thoughts and feelings when you feel the urge to smoke, so you can better understand what triggers those cravings and how to prepare for them?
I write a diary for my garden, maybe I can write one for myself too. But it's not like my garden, it's hard to write about feelings.

You're hesitant to express your feelings in a diary, that makes sense - writing about your garden is a more tangible experience. What do you think would make it easier for you to express your feelings in a diary, like your garden one?

If I could draw pictures or write just a few words, like the names of the flowers in my garden, it might be easier. Then it wouldn't feel like I'm writing about feelings, just about the day or the things I want to remember.

It sounds like you're thinking of incorporating some visual elements into your diary to make it feel more like your garden journal. Would drawing or writing about the things that bring you joy and comfort, like your garden, help you feel more connected to your non-smoking self?
Yes, it might. Drawing the flowers and birds that live in my garden makes me happy. Maybe if I draw the things that make me happy, I'll be happy too when I'm not smoking.

It sounds like you're connecting the joy you feel from your garden to your desire to quit smoking. Are you willing to experiment with drawing things that bring you joy, like flowers, birds, or even your family, and see if that helps you feel more positive when you're not smoking?
I smoke about 15 cigarettes a day. And I drink on weekends. When I drink, the smoking goes up a lot. My wife has been asking me to stop both.

You've been noticing that your smoking increases when you drink on weekends, and that's something your wife is concerned about. Can you think of a way to minimize your drinking on weekends if quitting smoking is a priority for you?
It's not that easy. My friends and I like to unwind on weekends. We go out, have some beers. If I quit drinking, I don't know what we'd do.
I get that it's hard to give up your weekend routine with friends, but what if you could find a way to unwind and have fun that doesn't involve drinking and smoking?
Like what? We usually just go to a bar, watch a game, or go out to eat. I don't know how to do anything else. We do have pool at home, but it's just not the same.

It sounds like you're missing out on the social aspect of going out with your friends, and the pool at home doesn't quite fill that void. Can you think of a way to incorporate your friends into a non-drinking, non-smoking activity that still feels like a fun night out?
We could play some board games or cards or something. Or take a group hike. But I don't know if my friends would go for that. We're not exactly the hiking type.

It sounds like you're exploring some ideas for social activities that don't involve drinking or smoking. What do you think about suggesting a trial group hike or game night with just a few close friends to start, rather than planning a big group outing?
That might work. Just a small test group, you know? See if it's something we can all do together. But what if they still want to go out to a bar afterwards?

You're thinking ahead to the potential challenges that might come up, like your friends wanting to go out to a bar after a game or hike night. Can you imagine having a plan in place for how you'd handle that situation?
I guess I could just meet them at the bar and have one drink, then head home. But it's not the same, you know? And my wife will still be worried about me drinking. This is all easier said than done.

You're feeling a bit overwhelmed by all the potential scenarios and your wife's concerns. Can you think of one person in your life who you feel would be supportive of your efforts to quit, and who you could reach out to if you're struggling?

My wife, actually. She's been on my case about this for a while, but she's also been understanding when I slip up. I mean, she's been trying to get me to quit for years. If I'm being honest, sometimes I think she's more motivated to get me to quit than I am myself.

It sounds like your wife has been a consistent source of support and motivation for you to quit smoking. Would you be open to sharing your struggles with her more specifically, so she can better understand what you're facing and offer help when you need it?

I don't know...she's always telling me to just quit, but she doesn't really get how hard it is. If I start telling her all the details, she'll just get frustrated with me for not quitting already. But maybe it's worth a shot.

You're feeling a bit uncertain about sharing your struggles with your wife, worried that she might get frustrated with you. How do you think it would change things if you approached the conversation with her as a team effort, rather than just asking for her support?

You know, that's actually a good point. We could work on this together. She could help me come up with a plan and be there when I need it. That way, it's not just me being on my own, trying to quit. It's like, we're in it together.

It sounds like having your wife as a partner in your quitting journey is a big motivator for you. Now, let's think about what a plan might look like - what specific steps could you take together to help you manage your cravings when you're feeling overwhelmed?

I think we could make a list of all the times I feel like smoking - like when I'm stressed at work, or when I'm arguing with my wife, or when I'm drinking with friends. Then we could come up with a plan for what to do instead of smoking in those situations. Maybe take a walk, or do some deep breathing exercises, or something.

That's a great idea to create a list of your triggers to help you prepare for those situations. Now, would you be open to using a small stress ball or fidget toy to keep your hands busy when you're feeling the urge to smoke?

That's not a bad idea. I could use something like that when I'm working and feeling stressed. It might help me focus on something else besides smoking. What's a good kind of stress ball, though? Something that won't get lost or make a mess?

You're considering the practicality of a stress ball, and that's a good point about it not getting lost or making a mess. Would you be comfortable with a small, discreet stress ball that can fit in your pocket or desk drawer?
Yeah, that sounds like it might actually work. A small one that I can carry with me would be okay, I think. But what if I'm still feeling really stressed and the stress ball doesn't work?

You're thinking about a safety net in case the stress ball doesn't help immediately. What if, as an alternative, you took a short break from your work to step outside and take a few deep breaths of fresh air?
I've been smoking for about 2 years. My husband and I are planning to have a baby soon. I know I need to stop.

You're feeling motivated to quit for the sake of your future baby - that's a powerful reason. How do you think having a baby will change your life, and how do you think your smoking habit might impact that?

Having a baby will definitely keep me on my toes, and I'm looking forward to it. I'm worried that my smoking will affect the baby's health, and my husband is really concerned too. He just wants the best for our kid.

It sounds like your husband's concern is really resonating with you, and that's creating a strong motivation to quit smoking. What do you think would happen if you were to have a baby and couldn't control your cravings, would you feel like you've let your husband and the baby down?
Yeah, I guess I would feel that way. It would be hard to see myself failing at this, especially since my husband is being so supportive.

It sounds like you're feeling a lot of pressure to be a good parent and to be successful in quitting smoking for your husband's sake. Can you tell me more about what a typical day is like for you, and when do you usually find yourself smoking?
It's just...work break times, I usually smoke then. And sometimes after dinner, just one cigarette, it's become a habit, I guess.

You've identified some specific situations where you tend to smoke, like work break times and after dinner. Have you ever tried replacing one of those activities, like taking a short walk during your work break, to see if it helps reduce your urge to smoke?
No, I haven't actually tried replacing it yet, I just knew I needed to stop. I don't know if I'm ready to try new things, it sounds like it might be hard.

You're hesitant to try new things because you think it might be hard. Can you think of a small step you could take, like just replacing one cigarette per day with a healthier habit, like chewing gum or taking a short walk, to see how that goes?
I guess I could try quitting one cigarette per day, but it would have to be the one after dinner. That's when I feel like I need a break the most.

You're identifying a specific time of day, after dinner, as a trigger to smoke a cigarette. How do you think your husband would react if you asked him to join you in taking a short walk together after dinner instead of smoking?
He'd probably be okay with it, he's really supportive. But sometimes I feel like it's my thing, you know? My way to unwind after a long day.

You feel like smoking is your way to unwind after a long day, and that's a significant part of why it's hard to quit. Would you be willing to consider exploring alternative ways to unwind, like talking to your husband about your feelings after dinner, rather than reaching for a cigarette?
I don't know...it's not just about feeling relaxed, it's also about the habit of it. Having a cigarette after dinner is just something I do, and it's hard to break that habit.

It sounds like you're struggling with the idea of breaking a long-standing habit. Can you think of a way to acknowledge and honor that habit by doing something similar after dinner, but without smoking, like having a cup of herbal tea or savoring a piece of fruit?
I guess that could work. Having a cup of herbal tea sounds pretty nice, actually. But I'd need to make sure it's a real tea, not just a way to distract myself from the craving.

You want to ensure the herbal tea is a meaningful alternative, not just a distraction. What if, instead of focusing on the tea itself, you think about creating a special moment after dinner, just like you do when you smoke a cigarette, but with the tea, to help you unwind and break the habit?
That sounds nice, actually. I could make the tea a special thing, like lighting some candles or something. That way, it's not just about the tea, it's about the ritual.

That sounds like a great start to creating a meaningful alternative. Can you think of what other sensory details you could add to this special tea ritual, like what kind of music or scents, to make it feel even more relaxing and enjoyable?
I like the idea of soft music, maybe some classical or nature sounds. And for scents, I could use some lavender or chamomile essential oil. It feels like it could be really calming.

That sounds like you're really starting to create a special and calming atmosphere with the herbal tea ritual. What if, when you feel the urge to smoke after dinner, you take a few moments to pause, light the candles, and engage your senses in this peaceful environment, rather than immediately reaching for a cigarette?
I think that could work. It feels like I'd be giving myself a chance to pause and choose something different. That's a good feeling. Maybe I can try that tonight.
You've taken a significant step forward in cultivating a healthier relationship with your habits – I'm confident in your ability to continue this positive momentum.
I work from home and since 2020 my smoking has more than doubled. I used to smoke 5 a day and now it's 18-20.

I can imagine how tough it must be to have your smoking habits change so much since working from home. What do you think is the main difference about working from home that's contributing to your increased smoking?
It's like I have no schedule and my brain gets really bored. I keep switching between tasks and that leads to procrastination, and I find myself smoking more during those times.

It sounds like your lack of structure and boredom are big contributors to your increased smoking, especially when you're switching between tasks.
