Can you imagine adding a short, achievable task to your list of things to do when you feel bored or stuck, just to help you stay engaged and smoke-free?
That's a good idea but I'm not sure what kind of task would be effective. I need something that's short and actually gets me to focus, you know?

You're looking for something short and focused to help you avoid smoking when you're feeling bored. Have you ever tried doing a few jumping jacks or a quick five-minute stretch when you feel stuck, just to get your blood flowing and your mind engaged?

I've done some exercise at home, but it's hard to commit to it when I'm feeling really stuck or procrastinating. Jumping jacks sound okay, but I'd need to remember to do them before I grab another cigarette.

You're right, remembering to do the jumping jacks can be a challenge. Would it be helpful to try setting a reminder on your phone to do a few jumping jacks every time you feel stuck or bored, so it becomes a more automatic habit?
I've set reminders for work-related tasks, but I've never thought of doing that for something like exercise. That might actually work, though, to get me out of my chair and moving.

You think setting reminders might be a good way to stay on track with exercise and distract yourself from smoking cravings. Would you be willing to try setting a few reminders a day, specifically for when you're feeling bored or stuck, to see how it goes?
That sounds doable. I can set reminders for, like, 10 or 15 minutes, and see how many times I can break the streak of smoking.
You're taking proactive steps towards a healthier habit – I wish you all the best on this journey.
I'm still a bit worried it won't stick, but I guess it's worth a try.

You're feeling a bit uncertain about whether this new strategy will help you stick to your quit goal. Can you think of a past time when you successfully stuck to a new routine or habit, and what might have helped you stay on track?

I remember when I started a new exercise routine a few years ago. I had a buddy who was doing it with me, so that helped keep me accountable. We'd schedule our workout times and check in with each other to make sure we were showing up. I think that social aspect was key to my success back then.

You're recalling a past success with accountability and social support - that's great insight. Would you consider finding a non-smoking buddy to share your quit journey with, or even just someone you can check in with regularly to report your progress?
That's not a bad idea. I have a colleague who's trying to quit as well, actually. We could support each other, that would be really helpful.
It sounds like having your colleague's support could be a great motivator for you. Would you feel comfortable having them hold you accountable and check in with you regularly to report your progress?
Yeah, I think that would actually work for me. I can be pretty self-critical, so knowing someone's expecting me to be on track would probably help me stay on track.
You seem to be feeling more confident and prepared to move forward with this plan.
I think so too. I'm still a bit anxious about quitting, but having a plan and someone to hold me accountable makes it feel more manageable.

It sounds like having a support system in place is really helping you feel more confident about quitting. Would you be open to thinking about specific times of the day when you tend to crave cigarettes, like your morning craving, and making a plan to tackle those moments with your colleague's support?

I can think about the times of day when I'm most likely to crave cigarettes. It's usually in the morning and after lunch, I think. If I know I'm going to check in with my colleague at those times, it might help me stay on track.

You're feeling more confident about tackling those specific times of day when cravings tend to hit. Would you be open to trying a "10-minute delay" technique when those cravings strike, where you delay smoking for 10 minutes and do something else instead, like taking a short walk or doing a quick exercise routine?
I used to smoke one and a half bundles of bidis a day. That's about 25. Now I'm down to 2 or 3. My doctor says I should stop completely.
That's a great step in reducing your bidis, going from 25 to 2 or 3 a day is a significant decrease. Can you tell me what you think triggered your desire to smoke even after your doctor's advice?

You know, I think it's hard to explain. Maybe it's just habit, I don't know. But one thing that's still really hard for me is in the mornings, having that one bidi after I wake up. That's the one I still really look forward to. The rest are just easier to give up because they're like, you know, in between tasks or when I'm stressed, but the morning one is just... it's just a ritual, I suppose.

It sounds like having that first bidi in the morning has become a deeply ingrained habit for you. Would you be open to trying a small change in your morning routine, like drinking a full glass of water as soon as you wake up, to see if it helps reduce your morning craving for a bidi?
I've tried drinking water, it's good for my health and all, but it doesn't really fill the gap that the bidi leaves. It's not the same, you know? Maybe we can think of something else to replace it?

It sounds like drinking water just didn't meet your needs for a morning ritual, and you're looking for something else to fill that gap. Would changing your environment when you feel the urge for a bidi, like going outside for some fresh air, help you manage your cravings in the morning?

Fresh air sounds nice, but it might not work when I'm in a hurry to get ready for work. And like I said, the morning bidi is really the one I struggle with. What if we try something to occupy my hands while I'm getting ready? That might help.

You're still struggling to give up that morning bidi, and you're open to trying something new to occupy your hands while getting ready. Would you be willing to try drawing or doodling something simple as soon as you wake up, like a small design or a phrase, to see if it helps distract you from craving a bidi?
Drawing sounds okay, I guess. But it's not like I'm a good artist or anything. What if I just end up making a mess and feeling frustrated?

It sounds like you're worried about feeling frustrated with drawing, which might make things worse. Can you imagine starting with something very simple, like drawing a line or a circle, and seeing if that helps you relax and focus on something other than craving a bidi?
I suppose it's worth a try. But if it doesn't work, what then? I don't want to be stuck with this... this drawing business.

You're willing to try drawing as a way to distract yourself from craving a bidi, and you're still a bit unsure if it will work. What do you think you'll do if you find yourself craving a bidi even after drawing for a bit, to help you stay on track with your goal to quit?

I don't know... Maybe I'll just take some deep breaths or think about why I wanted to quit in the first place? I've been doing pretty well so far, even though it's hard. Just getting to the point where I'm willing to try new things is a big step, right?

It sounds like you're feeling more motivated to try new things, like drawing and deep breathing, to help you quit bidis. Can you tell me more about what's making you feel hopeful about your ability to quit this time, like what's different from previous attempts?

I think I've finally realized that it's not about being perfect, it's about making progress. Last time, I was so focused on quitting all at once that I just got frustrated and gave up. This time, I've already made such big changes, reducing from 25 to 3 bidis a day. If I can just keep making small steps, I feel like I can actually do it.

It sounds like you've gained a lot of insight into what worked and didn't work for you in the past, and you're feeling more hopeful about this attempt. What if you were to create a daily plan for managing your cravings, like breaking it down into smaller steps, such as what you'll do when you wake up in the morning and feel the craving?

That sounds like a good idea. Okay, so in the morning, I'll draw something simple. And if I start to feel the craving, I'll take some deep breaths. And if I need something else, I can try to remember why I wanted to quit in the first place, like my breathing problems...

You're feeling more confident about using drawing, deep breathing, and remembering your reasons for quitting to manage cravings. What if we took it a step further and used the 4D technique specifically for morning cravings, to delay smoking for a few minutes, take deep breaths slowly, drink water, and do something else to distract yourself?

That sounds like a good system. But what if I'm already on my way to work and can't really take a few minutes to do all of that? Can't I just do the 4D technique during my short breaks at work instead?
You're thinking about adapting the 4D technique to fit your daily routine. Can you imagine what that would look like, and how it might help you manage cravings during short breaks at work?

I think I can do the 4D technique during my short breaks, like I can take a few slow breaths, drink some water from my bottle, and try to distract myself by looking at my phone or something. It might not be exactly the same, but it's close enough, and it could still help me get through those tough moments.

You've come up with a great adaptation of the 4D technique to fit your work schedule. How do you think you'll remember to use this technique during your short breaks, so it becomes a consistent habit?

I can set a reminder on my phone to take a short break and do the 4D thing, that way I'll remember to do it. And if I put a sticky note on my desk or something, that might help me too. Just a little reminder to myself to take a few deep breaths and get past the craving.

You're getting creative with reminders to help you stick to your 4D technique. What do you think about making those reminders a bit more personal, like writing down why you quit in the first place, or a motivational message to yourself?
I smoke about 20 cigarettes a day. I calculate I spend about 4000 rupees a month on cigarettes. My family is struggling and I feel guilty about it.

It sounds like the financial burden of smoking is really affecting you, and the guilt is weighing heavily on your mind because of your family's struggles. What do you think is the main reason you started smoking in the first place, and has that reason changed over time?

To be honest, I started smoking just because I had some friends who smoked and it was part of the scene. I didn't think about the cost back then. But over time, it's just become a habit, a thing I do without even thinking about it.

I think it's interesting that you mention smoking became a habit over time, almost like second nature. What situations or routines do you usually find yourself in that tend to trigger a cigarette, even though it's become a habit?

Long hours driving alone for deliveries, traffic gets to me and I just want something to calm me down. And after meals, it's just a routine I have. I don't even think about it anymore, just grab a cigarette and go.

It sounds like the stress of long drives in traffic is a big trigger for you, and after meals is more of a habitual thing for you. Since you mentioned stress relief as one of your motivations to quit, I'd like to suggest a simple technique to help you cope with stress while driving: can you try taking a short break and getting some fresh air or taking a few deep breaths when you feel stressed behind the wheel?

Fresh air sounds okay, I guess. But I'm not sure about deep breathing, I've never been into that sort of thing. How often would I have to take breaks, and would that really make a difference in my smoking habit?

You're willing to try getting fresh air as a way to cope with stress while driving, which is a great start. How do you think you could realistically incorporate short breaks into your delivery routes to make getting fresh air a regular habit?

I drive for a company that has designated rest stops where drivers can take breaks. I could use those places to get some fresh air. But to be honest, I'm not sure if I could take a break every time I feel stressed, it might disrupt my delivery schedule and affect my workload.

You're thinking about how your delivery schedule might be impacted by taking breaks, which makes sense. Can you try to imagine how your daily routine might change if you took just one extra break each day to get some fresh air, and what benefits that might bring to your stress levels and smoking habit?

I suppose if I take one extra break, I might not need to smoke as much to calm down. That could save me some money, and maybe I could use the time to stretch my legs too. But, I'm still smoking 20 cigarettes a day, so I'm not sure how much difference it would really make.

You're starting to see a potential benefit to taking breaks, and that's a great step. Since you mentioned you're smoking 20 cigarettes a day, can you imagine what this number would look like after just one week of incorporating those extra breaks into your daily routine?

I think if I cut down by even 2 or 3 cigarettes a day, that's like 10, 15 rupees saved. It's not a lot, but it's something. And if I can do that consistently, maybe I can work my way up to more savings, you know, like a thousand rupees a month. That would make a big difference for my family.

You're starting to see a tangible benefit to cutting down on cigarettes, and that's a great motivator. Can you imagine setting small, achievable goals for yourself, like saving a certain amount of money each week, which would help you stay on track and provide an extra incentive to keep reducing your smoking habit?

Yeah, that sounds like a good idea. If I save a thousand rupees a month for two months, that's like 20,000 rupees, which is a decent amount. But I need to make it concrete, like, if I save 100 rupees this week, and then 150 rupees the next week, and so on. That way I can see the progress and feel good about it.

You're getting more specific about your savings goals, which is really exciting. Since you mentioned you want to see the progress and feel good about it, have you considered writing down your savings goals and tracking them in a small notebook or on your phone to help you stay on track?

Yeah, that's not a bad idea. I've got a small notebook in my truck where I keep track of my delivery hours and expenses, so I could use that to track my savings too. Seeing the numbers add up in black and white would definitely help me stay motivated.

That sounds like a great idea to use your existing notebook to track your savings, and writing down your progress can be especially helpful in seeing the tangible benefits of quitting smoking. Can you imagine taking it a step further by also jotting down the days when you were able to avoid smoking after meals, which are a known trigger for you?

Yeah, that's a good idea too. If I can write down the days I resist the habit, that's like a bonus on top of my savings. It'll give me something to look at and feel proud about. Like, if I have a string of days where I don't smoke after meals, that's something to hold onto.

You're starting to see the connection between tracking your progress and staying motivated. Have you thought about rewarding yourself with something special when you reach a milestone, like a week without smoking after meals?

Yeah, that's a good idea. I've actually been thinking about it, and I think I could treat myself to a nice lunch at a restaurant with my family when I reach a certain goal. Maybe when I've saved 20,000 rupees, or like 30 days without smoking after meals. Something like that.

You've already come up with some great ideas for rewarding yourself, and it's clear that your family is a big motivator for you. Now, what if we could use your existing habit of tracking your savings to your advantage by also tracking your meals and identifying patterns around when you usually feel the urge to smoke after eating?

Yeah, that's not a bad idea. I could keep track of when I eat and when I feel like smoking afterwards. But, I'm not sure if I'll be able to change my habits that much. I've been eating the same way for years, and I always smoke after meals. It's just something I do.

You've been eating the same way for years, and yet you're willing to make changes now - that's already a step forward. Can you imagine getting up from the table right after meals, and doing something different like taking a short walk outside, before the urge to smoke even arises?
I only smoke at parties or when I'm drinking with friends, maybe 3-4 times a week. It's not like I'm a real smoker. But I've been having asthma attacks more often.

You feel like you're not a "real smoker" but the asthma attacks are really concerning you. How do you think you could handle situations where your friends are smoking and you're not, without feeling left out?

That's always the thing – feeling like I'll miss out. My friends will be like, "Come join us for a cigarette," and I don't want to say no, I want to fit in. But at the same time, I really don't want to have another asthma attack.

You're caught between wanting to fit in with your friends and wanting to protect your health. Have you ever thought about practicing a simple phrase to use in these situations, like "I'm trying to cut back on cigarettes, thanks for understanding"?

That sounds kind of cheesy, to be honest. I don't know if it would actually help me feel like I'm not missing out. I'm not sure I believe that saying something like that would really make a difference.

You're feeling a bit skeptical about using a simple phrase, which is totally normal. Can you imagine a situation where saying "no thanks" to a cigarette offer might actually be a relief for you, and not just a temporary discomfort?

I guess if I could really visualize myself saying no and feeling okay about it, that might help. Like, what would happen if I just said no and my friends didn't even notice? Would it really be that bad?

You're starting to see that saying no to a cigarette offer might not be as scary as you thought. What if we take it a step further and practice actually using that phrase in a low-stakes situation, like with a friend who doesn't smoke, to see how it feels?
That's a good idea, I suppose. I mean, it's not like my non-smoking friend will be judging me or anything. And it's not like I'm actually quitting or anything, it's just... testing it out.

You're starting to see this as a small step towards quitting, and that's a great place to be. Would you be willing to pick a specific day and situation to practice saying "no thanks" to a friend who doesn't smoke, just to see how it feels?
Yeah, I can do that. How about next Friday at work when Rachel asks me to grab coffee and a cigarette with her? It's not a big deal if I say no, and it would be good to get it out of the way.

You're feeling more confident about practicing saying no to a cigarette offer, especially in a low-stakes situation like next Friday at work with Rachel. How do you think you'll handle the potential pause or moment of hesitation that might come up when Rachel asks you to grab coffee and a cigarette?
I think I'll just pause for a second and take a deep breath, and then I'll say it. It's not that hard, right? I mean, I've practiced it in my head, so I should be okay.

It sounds like you're feeling more confident about saying no to a cigarette offer with Rachel next Friday. Would you be open to using a small distraction technique, like taking a few slow deep breaths, immediately after saying no to help manage any lingering urge to smoke?

Yeah, that's not a bad idea. If I have a second to compose myself, I could take a few slow breaths or something. That would help me feel more in control, I think. And then I could just focus on catching up with Rachel and enjoying the coffee.

You're feeling more in control when you take a few deep breaths, and that's a great feeling to build on. What happens to your morning craving when you wake up early, like on a Saturday, versus when you're getting ready for work on a weekday?

On a Saturday, my craving is definitely stronger. I think because I have more time to think about it, and I usually drink coffee or tea, so I associate those mornings with smoking. But on a weekday, I'm usually in a rush getting ready for work, so the craving isn't as bad, and I'm more focused on just getting out the door.

It sounds like you associate Saturday mornings with smoking due to the combination of having more time to think about it and your usual coffee or tea habit. Are you open to experimenting with making a small change to your Saturday morning routine, like drinking a cup of water or tea without smoking, to see if it can help reduce your craving?
Yeah, that's not a bad idea. I mean, water or a non-smoking tea is a pretty harmless swap. And if it helps me break the association, that would be good. I can try it and see how it goes.

It sounds like you're open to experimenting with a new Saturday morning routine. Can you imagine how it would feel to start your day without smoking, and then go about catching up with Rachel later in the day?

That sounds actually kind of nice. To start the day feeling more in control, and then knowing that I can handle a social situation like meeting up with Rachel without smoking. It's a small thing, but it feels like a big deal to me.

It sounds like you're really excited about the idea of starting your day without smoking and handling social situations like meeting up with Rachel without smoking. Who do you think could be that person who supports you and is aware of your efforts to quit smoking, so you can reach out to them when you need help or encouragement?

I think Rachel would be great for that. We've been friends for a while, and she's pretty understanding about my health stuff. We talk about it sometimes, and she's always been supportive. I could definitely count on her to be on my side if I'm trying to quit.

It sounds like you've thought about who could be that supportive person, and you've identified Rachel as a great choice. Can you imagine having a conversation with Rachel about your decision to quit smoking and asking her to be that person for you?
I smoke bidis. About 25 a day for the last 45 years. I've reduced a little now because the doctor here keeps telling me to.
You've been smoking bidis for 45 years, which is an impressive feat of habit. When you do smoke, what are the situations or times that make you feel like you really need to have a bidi?
Morning after waking up. It's the hardest to resist. And when I'm stressed about the farm, like if the crops aren't doing well or something.

You've identified morning cravings as particularly challenging for you, and also mentioned work stress. Does the idea of delaying your morning bidi by just 10 minutes, and doing something else in the meantime, feel like it might be helpful in reducing your desire to smoke?
Delaying by 10 minutes might help. But how? What do I do in that time?
You're curious about what to do during those 10 minutes, aren't you?
Yes that's right. Doing something to take my mind off it. Maybe some deep breathing exercises like the doctor here mentioned?

You're interested in deep breathing exercises, which can be a helpful tool to calm your body and mind. Are you willing to give it a try tomorrow morning, right after waking up, to see if it helps you delay smoking a bidi by that 10-minute window?
I'll try. But what if it doesn't work? I don't want to give false hopes.
You're worried about giving false hopes, but what if the 10-minute delay and deep breathing exercises actually do help, and then you can build on that success?
I guess it's worth a shot. Yes that's right. I'll try tomorrow morning.
It sounds like you're feeling more hopeful and empowered to take this first step – I wish you all the best.
Thank you.
You mentioned you'll try deep breathing exercises tomorrow morning - what do you plan to do if, after the 10-minute delay, you still feel like smoking a bidi?
I'll try to think of something else to do. Like going out to the fields, or checking my tools, or something.
You've found some alternative activities that might help distract you from the craving - are these activities something you do regularly, or is this a new idea for you?
I do some of those things already, like checking my tools, but not regularly. I usually just sit and smoke.

It sounds like you're not regularly engaging in these alternative activities, and they didn't seem to help much when you tried them. Would you be open to trying something new, like keeping your hands busy with a stress ball, to help manage your morning craving?
I don't know... it sounds like something silly to play with.
You're hesitant about the stress ball idea - what's making you think it sounds silly, is it the idea of playing with a toy or something else?
Just the idea of playing with a toy, like a child.
You're associating the stress ball with something childish, and that's making you hesitant - can you think of a way to make using a stress ball feel more adult and helpful for managing your cravings?
I guess if I'm using it to stay focused and calm while I'm working or thinking about my farm, it might be helpful.
Now that you've thought of using the stress ball in a more functional way, how do you think you could remind yourself to use it when you're feeling stressed or reaching for a cigarette?
I smoke bidis. One packet a day, about 15 to 16. I've been doing this since I was around 18 or 20.
You've been smoking bidis for about 7-8 years now, can you tell me what typically triggers you to smoke - is it when you're stressed, bored, or something else?
When my coworkers are smoking on site and they offer me one, that's when I usually take it. "Have one, yaar," they say, and it feels rude to refuse.

It sounds like social situations and peer pressure from your coworkers are making it hard for you to resist smoking. How do you feel when you say no to their offer and they seem to expect you to join in?
It's fine, nothing big. I just feel... ignored, like they're not really seeing me when I don't smoke along with them.

You feel like you're not being seen when you don't smoke with your coworkers, that's really painful for you. 

Are there any coworkers or people in your life who might actually see and support you in your decision to quit smoking, rather than just expecting you to join in?
My wife... she tries to help, but she's always stressed out about money too. She doesn't smoke, but she can't really understand what it's like to give up something that's a part of my life.

It sounds like your wife is trying to be supportive, but she's struggling with her own stress and might not fully understand what you're going through. Have you talked to her about how she could better support you in quitting smoking?
I haven't really talked to her about it, no. I don't want to burden her with my problems, and I don't want her to feel guilty about not being able to help more.

Since we're in the Planning phase, I need to focus on suggesting a technique to help with quitting. Considering your concerns about feeling ignored and not being seen, I think it's essential to have someone in your life who can provide emotional support.

Given your relationship with your wife, I'd like to ask: Would you be willing to have an open and honest conversation with her about your feelings and needs regarding quitting smoking, and see if she's open to learning more about how she can support you in a way that feels authentic to her?
I don't know... I've been doing this for so long, and every time I try to quit, it feels like I'm failing. I'm not sure if she'll think I'm weak or something if I ask for her help.

You feel like you've failed every time you try to quit, and that's making it hard to ask for your wife's help. What is one thing you've done in the past that might have worked a little bit, or made quitting feel slightly more manageable, even if it was just for a short time?

I think I was able to quit for a few days once, when I was working on a project away from my regular workplace. There, nobody smoked, so I didn't have that trigger. I managed for about three or four days, but then I went back to work and it was hard to resist.

Since you were able to quit for a few days when you were away from your regular workplace, it sounds like avoiding certain situations or environments might be helpful in quitting. What do you think about the idea of creating a list of places or situations that trigger your urge to smoke, so we can start working on strategies to avoid or cope with them?
That might help, I guess. But what about when I'm not at those places? What am I supposed to do then?

That's a fair concern about being in situations where you're not at your triggers. Would you be open to exploring other strategies to cope with cravings when you're away from those places, like the one I mentioned earlier about changing your environment when a craving occurs?
Yeah, maybe that could work. But what if I'm at home and I'm craving to smoke? That's when I get really anxious.
Since you feel anxious at home when you're craving to smoke, how do you think your body responds to anxiety - do you tend to move around or stay still?
I get restless, I pace around. I need something to do with my hands.

Since you mentioned needing something to do with your hands when you get restless, I was thinking that keeping your hands busy with activities like drawing or using a stress ball might help. Would you be willing to try drawing or doodling as a way to distract yourself from cravings?
I'm not exactly a good artist, but I guess it's worth a try. I could use a sketchbook or something.

You're feeling a bit uncertain about your artistic skills, but you're open to trying drawing as a way to distract yourself from cravings. Would you be willing to start with a simple exercise, like doodling some shapes or lines, to see if it helps you relax when you feel restless?
I suppose so, yeah. I mean, it's not like it's going to hurt or anything, right?
Since you're feeling a bit uncertain about your artistic skills, what do you think might help you feel more comfortable with the idea of doodling as a way to relax when you're craving?
I smoke bidis and eat paan. Started when I was around 25. Now it's maybe 7 or 8 bidis a day, down from more before.

You've been smoking bidis for about 7 years now, and you've noticed you're smoking fewer bidis than before. What do you usually do when you're feeling stressed or anxious, is smoking bidis a way to cope with those feelings?
It helps, yeah. If I'm feeling stressed or lonely, it's easy to grab a bidi. It's a habit, been doing it so long. But it's not helping my breathing, my daughter says.

You've acknowledged that smoking bidis helps you cope with stress and loneliness, but you're aware that it's not benefiting your health. Have you ever tried doing something physical, like going for a short walk, when you felt stressed or anxious, and if so, how did it make you feel?

One time my daughter asked me to walk with her, she's a big help, you know, trying to get me to do things. We went to the park, just around the block. It was okay, I guess. I felt a bit... lighter, maybe. But then I came back home and still had a bidi.

You mentioned that walking with your daughter helped you feel lighter, and it's interesting that you still had a bidi afterwards. When you walked with your daughter, did you notice if your cravings for a bidi decreased or changed at all during that time?

Yeah, I think they did, a bit. I wasn't thinking about smoking as much. But, it came back when I got home, and it was like I was waiting for it, you know, like I'd forgotten for a little while, but then it was there again.

You mentioned that walking with your daughter helped you feel lighter and reduced your cravings for a bit, but they came back when you got home. Have you ever tried practicing those same calming feelings right when you feel stressed or anxious, before you even reach for a bidi?

I don't know, it's hard to do something else when I wake up at 3 AM and can't sleep. That's when I first reach for a bidi, it's like a habit. Trying to do something else before that feels like I'm fighting it, and it's hard to change so much.

You're saying that trying to do something else when you wake up at 3 AM feels like you're fighting a habit, and it's hard to change so much. Have you ever thought about replacing the habit of reaching for a bidi with a small, gentle habit, like drinking a glass of water, which might help distract you from smoking?

Drinking water, yeah... that's not a bad idea. I've started doing it sometimes, just to see if it works. But it's hard to remember to do it when I wake up in the middle of the night. It's still a bit of a struggle.

You've already tried drinking water to help manage cravings and it seems to be a helpful strategy. Would you be willing to try setting a small reminder, like a sticky note on your nightstand, to help you remember to drink water as soon as you wake up at 3 AM?
A sticky note... yeah, that might help. It's a small thing, but maybe it's enough. My daughter puts sticky notes everywhere, I guess it could work.

You're open to the idea of using a sticky note to remind yourself to drink water. How do you think you would feel if you actually stuck to placing that note on your nightstand and following through with drinking a glass of water every time you woke up at 3 AM?
I don't know... it feels like a small thing, but maybe it's just the start. If I can do that, then maybe I can do other things too. It's hard to imagine, but it might be a start.
What do you think it would take for you to commit to placing that sticky note on your nightstand and following through with drinking a glass of water every time you woke up at 3 AM?

I think it would help if my daughter was there with me, reminding me. Or if I had someone to talk to, about my wife and everything. It's hard to remember to do something small when I'm thinking about everything else.

That's a beautiful connection with your daughter, it sounds like she's already a big support for you. Would you be open to talking to me about your wife and what's been going on, to see if that might help you feel more grounded and able to focus on those small reminders?

It's just... she was my everything, you know? We were together for so long, and then she passed away. I still wake up expecting to see her, and it's hard to sleep without her. It's like... everything feels so empty without her.

Given your grief and struggles with sleep, I'm wondering if it might be helpful to try writing down your thoughts and feelings about your wife every morning when you wake up at 3 AM, as a way to process your emotions and start to feel more grounded.
Writing things down... I've never tried that. It might help to get some of this out, yeah. Maybe I can write down things I'm grateful for, like my daughter, and that might help me feel a bit better.

It sounds like writing things down has already sparked some hope for you. Are you willing to give it a try and commit to writing down your thoughts and feelings about your wife, as well as things you're grateful for, every morning when you wake up at 3 AM?
I think so. Yeah, I'll try it. It's not a lot to do, and maybe it'll help. I'll put that sticky note with the water on the nightstand, and I'll write something down in the morning.
You've found a small step towards a new beginning, and I'm confident it will carry you forward.
My son is being treated here, so I thought I should see someone too. I smoke when I get angry or stressed. It's hard to explain.
It sounds like you've come to see me for your own health, and you're willing to address your smoking habit. How many cigarettes do you typically smoke per day?
Around 10, I guess. It's been 12 years now.
You've been smoking for 12 years now, that's a long time. What do you think would make quitting feel achievable to you, something you could really stick with?
My son... seeing him get treatment here, it made me think about how long I've been smoking and how it might be affecting him. Maybe if I stopped smoking, I'd be a better example for him.

That's really beautiful, talking about being a good example for your son. Would you be open to writing down some reasons why quitting smoking is so important to you, and maybe even sharing them with your son?
Maybe. I don't know... I'm not sure if he'd even care, or if he'd just get on my case about it like my wife does sometimes.
I understand you're feeling unsure about whether your son would care or be supportive. What would be the worst-case scenario for you if you tried to quit and your son wasn't supportive?
I don't know... I guess I'd just feel like I'm being a hypocrite or something. Like I'm trying to change, but my own son doesn't care.

It sounds like you're worried about feeling like a hypocrite if your son doesn't support you in quitting. Can you think of a way you could still share your reasons for quitting with your son and get his support, even if it's not immediate?

I could just start by telling him why I want to quit, and see how he reacts. Maybe he'll understand, or maybe he'll ask questions and we can talk about it together. It's not like I'm expecting him to quit too or anything.

It sounds like you're feeling more hopeful about sharing your reasons with your son. What if, in addition to talking with your son, you also created a small, personal list of reasons why quitting smoking is important to you, to refer back to when you're feeling uncertain?

That might be helpful, I guess. It's hard for me to think about right now, but if I had something to look at later, it might remind me why I wanted to quit in the first place. Like a checklist or something.

That resonates with me - you're looking for something to remind you of your reasons for quitting. Would you be open to writing down those reasons in a small notebook that you could carry with you, so it's always easily accessible?
Yeah, that sounds like a good idea. A notebook, yeah. I can do that.
Now that you have a notebook to remind yourself of your reasons for quitting, how will you use it to help you cope with urges to smoke when you're feeling stressed or angry?
I'll write down what's stressing me out or making me angry, and then I'll look at my reasons for quitting. See if it helps me calm down or something. It's worth a shot, right?
Since you've started using the notebook to write down your reasons and triggers, how will you make time each day to review and reflect on your entries, so it becomes a consistent habit?
I can just do it during my lunch break at school, or on the bus on my way home. It's not like I'm going to have a lot of free time or anything, but a few minutes a day should be doable.

You've got a plan in place for reviewing your notebook entries regularly. Now, what's the first thing you'd like to do when you feel that familiar stress or anger creeping in, before you even reach for your notebook?
I guess I'd like to take a few deep breaths first. See if I can calm down a bit before I try to figure out why I'm feeling that way.

You'd like to start by taking a few deep breaths, which is a great idea. Can you imagine incorporating this into a daily routine, like taking those deep breaths at the same time every day, so it becomes a habit?
Yeah, like taking a few deep breaths before my first cup of coffee in the morning. Before the kids wake up, before the chaos starts. Just a quiet minute to myself.

Now that you've thought of incorporating deep breathing into your morning routine, how do you think you'll make sure to carry it through on particularly stressful mornings when the kids might be fighting or waking up early?
Honestly, I don't know why I'm here. I don't think smoking is a big deal. I've been doing it for 25 years and I'm fine.

It sounds like you're feeling a bit uncertain about why you're here and questioning whether smoking is a problem for you. Can you tell me more about what makes you think you're fine with smoking after 25 years?

Well, my wife is always on my case about it, saying it's bad for my health and stuff, but I don't see any difference, you know? I'm still working, still taking care of myself, so I don't see why I need to quit.

It sounds like your wife's concerns about your health are a source of stress for you, and that's making you question the need to quit. Can you tell me more about what it's like when she brings it up, and how you usually respond to her concerns?

She gets on my nerves, you know? She's always nagging me, saying I'm going to die from lung cancer or something. I just tell her to calm down and that I'm not going to listen to her. I don't want to hear it.
It sounds like your wife's nagging is really frustrating for you. How does it make you feel when she brings up your smoking in front of others, like family or friends?

That's the worst. I get embarrassed, you know? Like, I'm a grown man, and she's still treating me like a kid. And in front of everyone else, it's like, what are they going to think? Like they're going to judge me or something.

It sounds like being judged by others, especially in public, makes you feel really embarrassed and self-conscious about your smoking. Can you tell me more about how you think others would judge you, and what that's based on?

I don't know, I just think they'd think I'm not a responsible person, you know? Like, if I can't even quit smoking with all the warnings and stuff, what else can't I do? They'd think I'm a failure or something.

It sounds like you're worried about what others will think of you if you can't quit smoking. Can you think of a time when you felt a strong urge to smoke, and what was happening in your life at that moment?

Yeah... like when I'm stuck in the shop for a long time with nothing to do. It's boring, you know? And I always reach for a cigarette then. Or after tea in the morning, when I'm still waking up, a cigarette tastes good. And when customers start haggling, I get stressed and a cigarette helps calm me down.

It sounds like boredom, morning routine, and stress at work are big triggers for you. When you're stuck in the shop with nothing to do, can you think of something else you could do with your hands and attention that doesn't involve cigarettes?
Uh, I guess I could work on some papers or something. But it's hard, you know? It's boring too. And it's not like I need to do it. I just sit there waiting for customers, you know?

Would you be open to trying to find a small, manageable task that you can do with your hands while you're waiting for customers, something that might make the time feel less boring and distract you from the urge to smoke?
I don't know... maybe. Like, I could organize the shelves or something. But it's not going to make a difference, is it? I'll still sit there and get bored, and I'll still need a cigarette.

You're thinking of organizing the shelves, that's a good start. Would you be willing to try that small task for just one day, without any pressure or expectation that it will solve everything, just to see how it goes?
I guess so. One day doesn't sound so bad. But if it doesn't work, I'm still going to smoke, okay?

Let's try to make organizing the shelves a bit more engaging. Would you consider making it a game for yourself, like seeing how quickly you can get it done or trying to find a new way to arrange the shelves?
A game, yeah... that might make it more interesting. But what if I'm still bored afterwards?

That's a valid concern about being bored afterwards. Would you be open to having a specific plan in place for what to do when you're feeling bored, like having a list of things you can do or someone to talk to?
Yeah, that could work. I mean, I could talk to my wife about it. She's always on my case about something, maybe she can come up with some ideas.

Now that you're considering talking to your wife about ways to manage boredom, would you feel more comfortable sharing your struggles with her about smoking, rather than just the general boredom issue?
Maybe. I don't know... it's just that she's always been on my case about it, I don't know if she'll ever understand why I still smoke.

I sense a bit of hesitation about sharing your struggles with your wife. Would you be more likely to open up to her if you felt like you could explain your reasons for smoking in a way that she would understand?
I've been smoking for 20 years and I've tried to quit many times. I don't know why I'm even here. It never works out.
It sounds like you're feeling frustrated and hopeless about quitting again. What's been going through your mind when you tried to quit in the past, but it didn't work out?

Well, it's like my mind just goes back to smoking. I'll be fine for a few days and then I'll have a bad day and just grab a cigarette to calm down. Before I know it, I've gone back to smoking a whole pack a day. It's like I'm stuck in this cycle.
It sounds like you feel like you're stuck in a cycle that's really hard to break. What would your life be like if you were able to break this cycle and stay smoke-free for good?

I don't know... it sounds cliche, but I guess I would feel more energetic and confident. I'd be able to run with my kid or play with my dog without getting winded. And I think it would be nice to be able to take a deep breath without coughing. It's the little things, you know?

You've shared some really beautiful reasons for wanting to quit, like being able to run with your kid and take a deep breath without coughing. Would you be open to trying something simple like deep breathing exercises to help calm your mind and body when you feel stressed or anxious?

I don't know... I've tried relaxation stuff before and I just can't seem to calm down. But I guess it's worth a shot. Just don't expect me to just sit there and breathe or anything like that. I need something a little more... tangible, I guess.

You've had some disappointing experiences with relaxation techniques before, and you're looking for something more tangible. Would you be open to trying something as simple as taking a short walk when you feel stressed or anxious, to see if that can help distract you from cravings and calm your body?

Yeah, okay... a walk might not be so bad. But what if the cravings are really strong? Like, when I'm at work and I'm stressed out... I don't know if I can just get up and walk around. My boss would think I'm crazy.

You're worried about what your boss might think, which shows you're thinking ahead and being responsible. Would you be open to finding a discreet way to take a short walk, like stepping outside for some fresh air or walking around the block during your lunch break?
I guess that's doable. But what about when I get home? I smoke after dinner almost every day... I don't know how to break that habit.

You're worried about breaking the habit of smoking after dinner, which is a challenging one for you. Would you be open to getting up from the table and doing something immediately after dinner, like brushing your teeth or chewing gum, to replace the habit of smoking with a healthier one?

I already brush my teeth, so that's not really an option. And chewing gum might help for a little while, but I know I'd just end up lighting a cigarette anyway. I need to find something else to do that's more meaningful, I guess.

Let's try to focus on finding a more meaningful activity to replace smoking after dinner. Would you be open to exploring a hobby or activity you've always enjoyed, but haven't made time for, that you could commit to doing after dinner every day?
I used to love drawing and painting, but I haven't done it in years. It's been collecting dust in the garage... I don't know if I'd even be any good anymore, but it might be worth a shot.

You're considering dusting off an old hobby that brings you joy, which is a great place to start. Would you be willing to set aside a specific time each day after dinner to work on your art, even just for a few minutes, to make it a consistent habit?
Okay... yeah, I can try that. Just 10 or 15 minutes a day, right after dinner, to start with. That sounds doable, I guess.
Now that you've committed to spending 10-15 minutes a day on your art, how do you plan to hold yourself accountable and make sure that time is dedicated to creating, rather than getting distracted?

I don't know... I'm not really the type to set reminders or all that organized. But maybe I could just leave my art stuff out in the living room, so it's in front of me after dinner. That way, I'd be reminded every day. And if I'm not in the mood, I could just put it away and try again the next night.

Let's focus on making your art time a consistent habit. What if, in addition to leaving your art supplies out in the living room, you also make a small agreement with a family member to hold you accountable and ask how your art project is going each evening?

My kid might actually be good for that. She's always checking in on me, making sure I'm doing okay. Yeah, that could work. She'd remind me to get back to my art and I'd be like, "Yeah, yeah, I'm working on it."
Now that you've decided on your family member holding you accountable, how can you reward yourself when you complete a full week of consistent art time after dinner?

I don't know... maybe something small, like a nice dinner or a movie night with my family. Or, I don't know, a new set of paints or something. Something to make it feel like I've accomplished something.

In the planning phase, I should focus on gently introducing one relevant coping strategy or technique based on their specific triggers and past attempts. I can now suggest one idea and check if they are open to it.

Therapist: Now that you've thought about rewarding yourself, what if we tried setting a small reward for staying smoke-free for a specific short period of time, say a day or two, to help build your confidence and motivation to continue?
Look, I work at a dhaba and everyone smokes here. It's part of the culture. I don't really want to quit, but the doctor insisted I come.
It sounds like working at a dhaba with a smoking culture might make quitting challenging for you. How long have you been working at the dhaba, and how long have you been smoking in general?
I work at this dhaba for like 10 years now. And, um, I started smoking around 22 years old.
It sounds like working at the dhaba has been a long-term part of your life. What's a typical day like for you at the dhaba, and how does smoking fit into your daily routine there?
It's pretty chill. I take orders, cook food, and chat with customers. After the rush, when it's all quiet, we all sit around and smoke. It's just a thing we do.

You've shared how smoking fits into your daily routine at the dhaba, especially after the rush when it's quiet. It sounds like a big part of the culture there. How does the idea of quitting smoking make you feel about your work and your relationships with your colleagues?
Honestly, it makes me feel...left out, I guess. Like, I'm not one of the guys anymore if I don't smoke with them. It's hard to explain. I don't know if I want to change that.

You're feeling left out, and that's a big part of why you're hesitant to quit smoking. Have you considered telling your colleagues about your decision to quit and asking them to support you, rather than feeling like you're changing your relationship with them?
I don't know...I don't want to be a bother. They're used to me smoking, and I don't think they'd understand. I've never tried quitting before, so I'm not even sure I can do it.

You feel like quitting would change your dynamics with your colleagues, and that's a big concern. Would you be open to practicing a simple phrase to politely decline a cigarette in a social situation, just to see how it feels?
I guess...I mean, it's not like I'm saying I want to quit forever right now, just trying something new. But, yeah, a phrase might help.

You're open to trying a phrase to politely decline a cigarette, but also still unsure about quitting altogether. How do you think your colleagues would react if you explained to them why you're trying to quit smoking, and that it has nothing to do with changing your friendship with them?
I don't know...They might think I'm being weak or something. But, I mean, I guess it's better than just quitting cold turkey and then being all distant.

You're feeling a bit uncertain about how your colleagues might react if you tell them about your quit attempt. Can you imagine a good way to address that concern, and how would you feel more confident in talking to them about it?
I guess if I explained it like it's just me trying to be healthier, and it's not about us or our habits, they might understand. And if they're really my friends, they should support me, right?

You've had a good insight about how to approach your colleagues about quitting smoking. Now, who in your life do you think would be most supportive if you told them you're trying to quit, and could you reach out to them for encouragement?
My wife, maybe. She's always been pretty supportive, and she's been on me about quitting for years. I never thought I'd actually do it, so maybe she'll be happy if I finally try.

It sounds like your wife could be a great source of support in your quit attempt. Would you feel more comfortable reaching out to her as soon as you decide to quit, or would it be helpful to talk to her about your plans first?

I don't know...I don't want to get her hopes up if I end up not going through with it. Maybe I should talk to her a bit more about it, you know, see where I am, and then we can figure it out together.

You're worried about building up your wife's expectations, and you want to approach the conversation with her carefully. Would it be helpful to keep a journal or log of your thoughts and feelings about quitting, so we can review it together and identify patterns, like times when you feel more confident about quitting or when your concerns about your wife's expectations come up?
A journal might help me get everything straight in my head. It's hard to keep track of all the reasons I want to quit and all the reasons I don't right now.

A journal might be really helpful in clarifying your thoughts and feelings about quitting. What do you think you'd like to write down first, the reasons you want to quit or the reasons you're worried about quitting?
I think I'll start with the reasons I'm worried about quitting. It's easier to see what's holding me back if I write it down, and maybe then I can think about how to deal with those things.

You're planning to write down your concerns about quitting, which can be a great way to clarify things. What do you think might happen if you were to write down the reasons you actually want to quit, alongside the reasons you're worried about quitting?
I quit for about 3 months last year. Then a really bad week at work happened and I started again. Now I'm back to 15 a day.
I remember you said you quit for 3 months last year, but then a tough week at work led you to start again. What exactly was it about that week at work that triggered you to go back to smoking?

A lot of deadlines were due, and my team was under a lot of pressure. I had to work long hours and make some tough calls. It was stressful and I just felt overwhelmed, and somehow smoking just became a habit again.
Given your experience with work stress triggering your smoking habit, would you be open to trying something like using a stress ball to keep your hands busy when you feel overwhelmed at work?
I've seen those stress balls before, but I'm not sure it would work for me. I think it's about more than just having something to fidget with.
You feel it's about more than just having something to fidget with, so let's explore what specifically it is for you that makes smoking feel like a solution to your stress and overwhelm.

It's hard to put into words, but it's like smoking is a way for me to take a break, you know? It's a excuse to get out of the room or step away from my desk, even if it's just for a minute. And sometimes, when I'm really stressed, I just feel like I need a physical way to shake it off, I guess.

You mentioned that smoking is a way to take a break and shake off stress, so can you think of other ways you could take a break or release tension that don't involve smoking, like going for a short walk or doing some stretching exercises?

I guess so, yeah. Going for a walk is a good idea. I like that. But it's just that when I'm in the middle of a meeting or something, it's hard to excuse myself to go outside. And sometimes I feel like my coworkers would think it's weird if I started doing stretching exercises at my desk. I don't know, it just feels kinda awkward.

You mentioned that excusing yourself to go outside might be difficult, especially during meetings, so what do you think about having a quick stretch or taking a few deep breaths right at your desk instead?
That might be doable. I can try that, I guess. But what if people think I'm not paying attention or that I'm being unprofessional? I don't know if I'm comfortable doing that in front of everyone.

You're worried about what others might think of you taking a break at your desk, do you think it would be helpful to talk to your coworkers about your quit plan and reassure them that you're taking care of yourself?
Yeah, maybe that would help. It's not like I'm asking them to do anything, just that I need to take care of myself sometimes. But...I don't know, it still feels kinda personal, you know?

You feel like talking to your coworkers might be a bit too personal, so what do you think about having a small support person, someone you trust, who can help keep you accountable and provide encouragement when you need it?

I've got a friend who's also trying to quit, but he's been struggling too. Maybe having both of us support each other could work. It would be nice to have someone to talk to who understands what I'm going through.

That's a great connection to make with your friend, having someone who understands what you're going through can be really helpful. Now, what specific role do you think your friend could play in supporting you in quitting smoking?
Well, he could send me reminders to stay on track, or just check in with me every day to see how I'm doing. Sometimes just knowing someone is thinking about me can make a big difference.

Now that you've thought about how your friend could support you, it sounds like having reminders and daily check-ins could be really helpful. Would you be willing to ask your friend to send you a specific message or text every day to help keep you accountable and motivated?
That's a good idea. Yeah, I can ask him to send me something simple like "How's it going?" or "Stay strong". That way, I'll know he's thinking about me and it'll give me something to focus on.

Now that you've thought of a way to stay connected with your friend through daily check-ins, what do you think would make these reminders even more effective in helping you stay on track with quitting smoking?
If I had a specific goal or achievement for each day, like a certain number of hours without smoking, it would give me something to work towards and make the reminders feel more meaningful.

Now that you've thought about having daily goals to work towards, would you be willing to set a specific, achievable goal for your first day of quitting, like staying smoke-free for a certain number of hours?
Yeah, I can do that. How about I try to make it to lunchtime without smoking? That's at least 5 hours.
Given that you've set a goal to make it to lunchtime without smoking, how do you plan to handle the urge that often hits you in the morning when you first start your workday?
I smoke because it calms me down when I feel anxious. I know it makes things worse but I can't stop.
It sounds like you're using smoking as a way to calm your anxiety. How long have you been smoking?
5 years, I guess. It's just something I do.
You mentioned it's just something you do, but I want to clarify: how many cigarettes do you typically smoke in a day?
Um, 8 or 10. I don't really keep track.
You mentioned you smoke 8 or 10 cigarettes a day, but what type of cigarettes do you usually smoke, are they menthol or regular?
Regular, I don't know, just... regular cigarettes.

You've been smoking regular cigarettes for years, and I'm getting a sense that it's become a habitual part of your routine. Would you be open to getting rid of your cigarettes and other smoking-related items at home for a few weeks to help break that habit?
I... don't know. That sounds hard. What if I need one and it's not there?

You're worried that being without cigarettes at home might make it harder for you to resist the urge to smoke. Can you think of any small steps you could take instead, like storing your cigarettes in a less accessible place, to test out how that feels?
I guess that might be doable. But what about when I'm at my friends' places or my boyfriend's? They all smoke too.

You're concerned about social situations where your friends and boyfriend smoke. What do you think would happen if you politely told them you're trying to quit smoking and didn't want to be around cigarettes during this time?
They'd just think it's weird, or try to get me to join them. They wouldn't really understand. Plus, it's hard to say no to them. They smoke with me all the time.

You're feeling a bit anxious about saying no to your friends and feeling like they might not understand. Would it help to have someone in your life who knows about your quit plan and can offer you support and encouragement in tough situations?
My boyfriend's really understanding, he's always trying to help me. But even with him, it's hard to ask for help when I'm feeling overwhelmed. I feel like I'm already a burden to him with my anxiety.

Considering your anxiety often gets the better of you when you're feeling overwhelmed, would it be helpful to have a simple phrase or word that you can say to your boyfriend, like "I need a minute," to signal to him that you're feeling overwhelmed and need some space?
That could work, I guess. But what if he thinks I'm just being anxious again and tries to talk me out of it? I don't want to be that person all the time.

You're feeling a bit uncertain about how your boyfriend will react if you ask for space. Would it help to practice saying that simple phrase, "I need a minute," with me in this session, so you can feel more comfortable using it in real-life situations?
Maybe. It's just weird saying it out loud to you, like I'm practicing in front of someone or something.

You're feeling a bit self-conscious about practicing the phrase with me, and it's understandable since you're not used to saying things out loud like that. Would you be willing to try saying "I need a minute" out loud just this once, and see how it feels?
I need a minute. *pauses* Yeah, it doesn't sound so bad, I guess.

Now that you've gotten a chance to practice saying "I need a minute" out loud, let's talk about what happens when you start to feel anxious or overwhelmed in social situations, like when you're with friends or at a party.
I... get really uncomfortable. I start to feel like I'm going to freak out, like I'm going to embarrass myself in front of everyone. That's usually when I reach for a cigarette.

You often smoke when you feel like you're going to freak out in social situations, but let's think about what could happen if you had a different way to calm down in those moments - can you imagine taking a few deep breaths instead of reaching for a cigarette?
I had quit for 2 full years. Then I went to a party, had a few drinks, and one of my friends offered a cigarette. That was 6 months ago. Now I'm back to 10-15 a day.
That must have been a really tough situation to be in at the party, feeling like you were being pulled back into old habits. What was going through your mind when your friend offered you a cigarette?

Honestly, it was just a split second decision. I was in a social situation, and it felt like this one little thing, this one cigarette, was the easiest way to fit in and not rock the boat. I didn't really think about it, I just did it.

It sounds like when you're in a social situation, you feel like smoking can make things easier and help you fit in. What's the thought or feeling that's driving you to want to quit for good this time, beyond just getting through this one situation?

It's just... I feel like I've let myself down. I know I can quit, I've done it before, but it always slips away. And I'm tired of feeling like I'm not in control. Plus, my girlfriend's always on my case about it, and it's just getting old.

You've been in this situation before, quit for 2 years and then relapsed. What did you do during that 2-year period that you think might help you quit again, and how can you build on that this time around?

During the 2 years I quit, I found out I actually enjoyed hiking and biking. I started doing that a lot, and it really helped me stay active and feel good about myself. This time around, I was thinking maybe I could try to get back into that, and maybe even find some new activities that'll make quitting easier. But it's hard, you know, when you're used to smoking in social situations...

You've had success with hiking and biking before, which really helped you stay active and feel good about yourself. Would you be open to making a specific plan with me to incorporate more of those activities into your daily routine, starting immediately after we quit?

Yeah, I can do that. I mean, it sounds like a good idea. But I'm not sure I'm ready to make a plan just yet. Can we, like, take a few days to think about it? I don't want to promise something and then not follow through.

Let's break down what we've discussed so far about your struggles with social situations and how much you enjoy activities like hiking and biking. You mentioned wanting to find new activities that'll make quitting easier. Can you think of any specific activities or hobbies you'd like to try that don't involve smoking?

To be honest, I've always wanted to learn how to play the guitar. I've had a guitar just sitting around for years, but I've never had the time. I think that would be a pretty cool thing to try, but it's just something else to add to my plate, you know?
Given that you've expressed interest in learning to play the guitar, would you be willing to schedule some time this week to pick up where you left off, even if it's just a short practice session?

I guess that would be a good idea. But only if my girlfriend can be there too. She's always been supportive of me quitting, and it might be good for her to see me making an effort to try something new.

It sounds like your girlfriend's support means a lot to you, and having her involved in trying new things could really help motivate you to stay on track. Would you be open to exploring ways to make your guitar practice sessions, and other activities, a regular part of your daily routine, even if it's just 15-20 minutes a day?

Yeah, that sounds like a good starting point. I mean, 15-20 minutes a day, that's doable. Plus, it's not just about the guitar, it's about having something to look forward to, you know? Something that's not smoking.
Given your desire to have something to look forward to, would you be open to setting a reward for yourself when you reach a smoke-free milestone, like a week without smoking?

Rewarding myself sounds like a good way to stay motivated. Let me think for a second... if I can make it to a week without smoking, I'd like to treat myself to a new guitar pick. I've been eyeing this really cool one that's got a nice finish to it.

That sounds like a great idea about the new guitar pick, and it's already started to give you something to look forward to. Would you be willing to share how you envision celebrating a smoke-free week, and what that might look like beyond just the guitar pick?

If I make it to a smoke-free week, I think I'd like to have a little celebration with my girlfriend. Maybe we could go out for dinner or see a movie, something low-key. And then, I could surprise her by taking her to this new hiking trail that just opened up near our house. It's something we've been meaning to check out, but haven't had a chance yet.

You've already thought of some great ways to celebrate a smoke-free week, like taking your girlfriend out for dinner or a movie, and then surprising her with a hike on that new trail. How can we build on the idea of delaying the response to a craving by setting a small goal to wait, say 10 minutes, before acting on it when you feel the urge to smoke?

I think that's doable, but it's hard for me to put a time limit on it. Like, if I'm at a party and someone offers me a cigarette, I don't know if I can just say no right then and there. I think I need to find ways to make the delay feel more manageable, maybe by distracting myself with something else or talking to my girlfriend about how I'm feeling.

You're saying that putting a time limit on it feels hard, especially in social situations. Would you be open to exploring ways to use a physical activity like taking a short walk as a way to distract yourself from the craving and make the delay feel more manageable?

Taking a short walk might actually help. I mean, it's not too much to ask to just get up and move around for a minute or two. And it's not like I'm committing to anything, it's just a minute or two, right?

You're feeling more open to the idea of taking a short walk to manage cravings. How might you incorporate this into your daily routine, especially in situations where you know you're more likely to crave cigarettes, like parties or social gatherings?
I've tried to quit 3 times. The longest I went was 3 months. But I just can't seem to stay quit. My daughter is growing up and I don't want her to see me like this.
You've mentioned that your daughter is growing up and you don't want her to see you like this – what does that mean to you, what are you worried she'll see?

I don't know... it's just that my husband smokes too, and our friends all do, but when I'm around my daughter, I want to be a good role model. I want her to see me healthy and strong. Seeing me smoke all the time, it's just... it's not what I want her to see.

You're really motivated by the thought of being a good role model for your daughter - that's a powerful reason to quit. What's it like when you're around your husband and friends who smoke, does that make it harder for you to stay on track with quitting?

It's hard, yeah. When they're all smoking, it's just so normal for me to light up too. And they're all supportive, they say I'm doing great and they'll quit with me, but it doesn't feel like it. It feels like they're just being nice, you know?

It sounds like being around your husband and friends who smoke makes it really difficult for you to stay on track with quitting. Do you think having someone in your life who is also quitting, and who can support you in those tough moments, might make a big difference?

I don't know... I've asked my husband to quit before and he's tried, but he gives up easily. I don't know if he'd be able to help me or make me feel worse. It's hard to ask someone for support when I know they're not really committed to quitting themselves.

Since we're in the Planning phase, I need to focus on introducing a relevant coping strategy or technique based on the patient's specific triggers and past attempts. Here's a question that fits this phase:

Considering your past experiences quitting, what did you find helpful, even if it was just for a short time, that might work again in your current situation?

Well, during my longest quit attempt, I actually found that using gum and going for short walks really helped me manage stress and cravings. It was just something to distract me from wanting to smoke. But I think what really fell apart was when I got into high-stress meetings at work. I just couldn't find a way to calm down and wait out the cravings.

You've found that using gum and short walks helped you manage stress and cravings in the past, which is great. Now, considering those high-stress meetings at work were a challenge for you, do you think carrying some gum or a small stress-relief tool with you during those meetings might be helpful?

Yeah, that's not a bad idea. I could try keeping some gum in my bag or even a small stress ball or something. But what about when I'm driving? That's still a big trigger for me, and I don't think I can just pull over to take a walk in the middle of the road.

You mentioned driving is a big trigger for you, and it can be tricky to manage cravings while on the road. Can you think of any other ways you could prepare or distract yourself before getting into the car or while driving that might help you stay on track?

I've thought about listening to music or audiobooks while I drive, but I'm not sure if that would really help. I usually get a bunch of work calls or listen to the news, so I'm stuck with the stress and anxiety anyway. But maybe I could try to plan my route ahead of time, that way I could avoid areas with a lot of traffic and reduce my stress level before I even get in the car.

Considering you've thought about listening to music or audiobooks while driving, but you're not sure if that would really help, have you ever tried using music or any other form of audio to distract you from cravings in other situations, like when you're at home?

Actually, yeah... when I'm at home, I sometimes listen to calming music or nature sounds to help me wind down and relax. It does help take my mind off things, so maybe carrying an MP3 player or phone with some of that music in the car could work too.

Since you've found that listening to calming music or nature sounds helps you relax at home, do you think it could help you stay focused on not smoking during those high-stress meetings at work, or even while driving?

Yeah, that's a great idea. I never thought of listening to calming music while I'm driving, but it might actually help. I could try making a playlist with some soothing music and listen to it during meetings and while I'm driving. It's worth a shot, right?

You're open to trying calming music while driving, and it sounds like it might be a helpful distraction. Now, let's think about how you can make sure you actually use the music when you need it - do you think you could set a reminder or create a habit of putting on your calming music playlist before you get in the car or start work?

Yeah, a reminder sounds like a good idea. I could set an alarm on my phone to remind me to put on the music before I get in the car or before a big meeting. Or maybe I could make it a habit to put it on every time I get in the car, so it becomes second nature. That way, even if I'm stressed or distracted, I'll automatically reach for the music.

You've already thought of setting a reminder or making it a habit to put on the music before driving or meetings. How do you think you can make sure you actually listen to the music when you're feeling stressed or overwhelmed?

I guess what I need to do is make a deal with myself to always put on the music as soon as I think about smoking. Like, if I'm starting to feel a craving, I tell myself to put in the earbuds and turn on the music instead. That way, I'm not just putting on the music out of habit, but also using it as a way to resist the craving.

I love how you're thinking about using the music to resist cravings. Now, let's think about what you'll do with those 10 minutes while listening to music - how can you make sure you're not just delaying smoking, but actually moving closer to your goal of quitting?

I was thinking that during the 10 minutes, I could take some deep breaths, focus on my surroundings, and remind myself why I'm quitting. I could also try to think of other ways to cope with my stress instead of smoking. Like, if I'm in a meeting, I could take notes or ask questions to stay engaged. If I'm driving, I could take a different route or call a friend to talk.

Considering your plan to use the 10 minutes to take deep breaths, focus on your surroundings, and remind yourself why you're quitting, would you be willing to also keep a small notebook with you to jot down some of those thoughts and reminders, so you can refer back to them later when you need a boost of motivation?
I chew gutkha, not cigarettes. About 8 to 10 pouches a day for the last 7 years. My mouth has developed some kind of white patch and the doctor is worried.

So you've been chewing gutkha for 7 years and are concerned about the white patch on your mouth - that must be pretty worrying for you. 

What's a typical day like for you in terms of when and where you usually chew gutkha?

Yeah, it's scary. A typical day for me is driving my auto-rickshaw. I usually chew gutkha during long shifts, when I'm stuck at red lights, or when I have some downtime between trips. It helps me stay awake and keeps my mouth busy, you know?

Since you often chew gutkha during downtime, stuck at red lights, or between trips, I'd like to try something that might help you stay occupied during these times. Would you be willing to try carrying a small bottle of water with you and taking a sip every time you're stuck at a red light or feeling bored?

Taking water... I mean, I do drink water, but it's not like I have a specific habit of drinking at those moments. I'd try it, but what if I forget? I'm always on the move, it's hard to keep track. And gutkha is already there, it's just a pouch.

You're worried that you might forget to drink water when you're moving around. Do you think having a small label or reminder on your water bottle might help you remember to take sips during those times?

A label... I don't know, that might help. But what if it gets in the way or falls off? And what about when I have a lot of trips one after another? It's not always easy to drink water and then have to deal with the next passenger right away.

You're worried that a label might get in the way or fall off, and that it might be difficult to drink water when you have back-to-back trips. How do you think you could make drinking water a more automatic habit, like chewing gutkha is for you now?

I guess if I put the water bottle in a specific place on my dashboard, like next to my lighter, I might be more likely to pick it up. But then I'd have to remember to refill it sometimes, and I don't always get to stop for a long time.

You're thinking of putting the water bottle next to your lighter on the dashboard to make it easier to remember to drink. What if we took it a step further and had you set a reminder on your phone for when you know you'll be stuck in traffic or doing a series of short trips, so you can drink water at those specific times?

Reminders on my phone... that's a good idea. I do have a lot of habits tied to my phone, like checking the time or responding to messages. Maybe if I set a reminder to drink water at certain times, I'll start to associate drinking water with those habits.
