You think setting reminders on your phone might help you associate drinking water with certain habits, like checking the time. How can you make sure you won't just ignore the reminders, and actually take a sip of water when they go off?

I guess I need to make it a bit more inconvenient to ignore the reminders. Like, I could set it to make a sound that's really loud, or maybe even vibrate the phone really strongly. That way, when it goes off, I won't be able to just glance at it and forget.

You're thinking of setting your phone to make a loud sound or vibrate strongly to help you remember to drink water. How do you think you'll feel when your phone starts making that noise or vibrating, and what will you do with that feeling?

When it happens, I'll probably feel a bit annoyed, to be honest. It's gonna be like, jolted out of my usual state. But maybe I can use that feeling to my advantage. Instead of getting annoyed, I can just take a sip of water and think, "Okay, I'm doing this." Maybe that can help make it a habit.

You're feeling a bit hopeful about turning your annoyance into a cue to drink water. How can you make sure you're not just drinking water out of habit, but also using it as a way to distract yourself from cravings when you feel that annoying jolt?

I mean, I'll still have cravings for gutkha when the reminder goes off. But if I can associate drinking water with those moments, maybe it can become a substitute... not the same kind of fix, but at least something to do with my hands and my mouth. Plus, it's free and good for me, so...

You're thinking that drinking water might become a helpful substitute for gutkha when you feel cravings. Can you imagine yourself staying with the feeling of craving instead of immediately reaching for gutkha or water, and see if that changes your experience?

I never thought of just sitting with the craving. It's like, I always try to get rid of it or distract myself from it. But if I stay with it... I don't know, maybe it'll start to feel less intense or maybe I'll realize it's not as important as I think it is.

You're thinking that just sitting with the craving might help it feel less intense or become less important. How can you start practicing this sitting-with-the-craving approach when you feel a strong urge for gutkha, like when you're stuck at a red light?

I can start by just noticing the feeling in my mouth, I guess. When I feel the craving coming on, I'll just sit with it without doing anything about it. Not even drinking water. Just feel the wanting and the restlessness. See what happens. It might be hard at first, but if I can just sit with it for a few seconds...

You're feeling hopeful about sitting with the craving instead of immediately acting on it. How will you make sure you're not avoiding the feeling of craving, but actually leaning into it and exploring what's behind it?

I think that's a good question. I don't want to just be avoiding the craving, but actually understanding it. Maybe I can try to label it in my head - "I'm feeling restless and I want a fix" or something like that. And then I'll just sit with that feeling and see what's driving it. Is it boredom, or is it something else?

Now that you're thinking of labeling and understanding the craving, you've taken a big step. Can you imagine how it would feel to actually label the craving in your head and then pause for a moment to see how that changes your experience?
I started smoking 2 years ago in college. My friends all do it. I'm on the cricket team and my stamina is getting really bad now.
You mentioned starting to smoke in college with friends, can you tell me more about how being part of the cricket team and your friends who smoke fit into your daily routine?

We usually hang out after college, have a few cigarettes, and catch up on our day. It's pretty normal for us. The team's performance has been declining lately, and our coach mentioned something about how I'm smoking too much. I don't know, maybe it's affecting me on the field or something.

I sense that socializing with your friends is an important part of your day, and cigarettes have become a habit in those situations. Would it be helpful to try keeping your hands busy with an activity like drawing or writing during those times, just to see if it makes a difference?
That might be kinda weird, you know, drawing or writing in front of them. They'd probably think I'm weird or something. But, if it means I can stay on the team, I guess I could try it.
You're feeling hesitant about trying something new in front of your friends. What do you think might make it more comfortable for you to try drawing or writing with your friends?

Maybe if you told our coach about it and said it's part of our training or something, it wouldn't be so embarrassing. It's not like I'd want to be a different person, but if it helps the team, I'm willing to try.
Now that you're considering trying something new with your friends, how do you think your coach's reaction to this idea would impact your willingness to give it a try?

If the coach says it's a good idea and it's for the team, I'd be more likely to give it a shot. He's always talking about how we need to work together and improve, so if he's behind it, I'd feel more confident. Plus, it wouldn't be just about me being weird; it'd be something we're all doing together.
That's really helpful for you to feel more confident about trying something new if your coach is on board. Would you be willing to talk to your coach about this idea and see how they react?
Yeah, I can do that. I'll tell him it's not gonna work, but I'll try to keep an open mind. Maybe he can give me some tips on how to, you know, stay focused without smoking.

Since you're open to the idea of talking to your coach and wanting to stay focused without smoking, I think it might be helpful to practice a simple phrase to say when someone offers you a cigarette, especially in social situations. Would you be willing to try saying "no, thanks" and reminding yourself why you quit when someone invites you to smoke?
It sounds kinda simple, but I don't know if I can just say no without feeling weird. What if they ask me why, and I don't have a good reason?

That's a valid concern about not having a good reason to say no, and it can be tough to come up with an answer on the spot. Can you think of any reasons related to your team or goals that you could use when someone asks why you're not smoking?

I can tell them it's because I want to improve my game for the team. That way, I'm not lying or anything, and it's related to why I want to quit in the first place. Maybe it'll even sound cool to them or something.
Now that you have a reason that feels authentic to you and related to your goals, can you imagine how you might respond if someone asks you to smoke in a social situation?

I'd say something like, "Nah, I'm trying to stay sharp for the team. I want to make sure I'm doing everything I can to help us win." It sounds pretty good in my head, but what if they don't believe me or think I'm being fake?

You've got a good response ready in case someone asks you to smoke in a social situation. What would it take to boost your confidence in saying no and standing by your decision, even if others don't believe you?

If you think the coach is behind me trying to quit, that would help a lot. If he's on my side, it'd show that it's not just me being weird, that it's something we're all working towards together. That would make me feel more confident, I think.
I want to acknowledge how much it means to you to have the coach's support. If you were to talk to your coach about your goals and ask for their encouragement, what would you say to them?

I'd say something like, "Coach, I know we've been struggling lately, and I want to be a part of the solution. I'm trying to quit smoking to improve my game, and I was wondering if you could help me with that. Your support would mean a lot to me, and I'd really appreciate it if you could be on my side."

You've come up with a plan to talk to your coach about your goals. How do you think it would feel to have your coach's support and encouragement in place before you need to say no to a cigarette in a social situation?
It'd be a huge weight off my shoulders, I think. Knowing the coach has my back would give me so much more confidence to say no to cigarettes, and it would feel like I've got a lot less to lose.

Given that we're in the Planning phase, I should focus on gently introducing a relevant coping strategy or technique. Considering the patient's recent conversation about having the coach's support, I'll suggest a technique that aligns with their confidence boost. 

Therapist: That sounds like a great feeling - having the coach's support would be a big confidence booster. How about practicing a simple phrase to remind yourself of your commitment to quitting whenever you feel uncertain or in a high-risk situation, like when someone offers you a cigarette?
My son brought me here. I smoke 4-5 bidis a day and eat paan. I've been doing this for 30 years. I don't think I can stop at this age.

That's a lot of responsibility your son is taking by bringing you here - it's clear he cares about you. What are some of the situations or emotions that make you reach for a bidi, besides just the habit itself?
It's after I finish cooking, you know, when I have some free time, I usually reach for a bidi. And sometimes in the afternoons when I'm resting, I just get used to having one.
Now that we've identified those situations, how do you think we could break the automatic link between finishing cooking and reaching for a bidi?
I don't know... maybe find something else to do after cooking, like... gardening or something. But it's hard, because that's just what I've always done.

It sounds like breaking your routine after cooking is a big challenge for you. Would you be willing to try doing something as simple as taking a short walk around the block after you finish cooking, just to see if it helps you break the link between cooking and smoking?
A walk might be nice... but it's getting colder in the evenings, I don't know if I'm up for it.
That's understandable about the cold weather, but how might taking a short walk on a warmer morning or afternoon, when you're usually resting, help you feel more energized and less inclined to smoke?
Maybe I'd feel tired later, that's what I'm worried about... can't keep up with my routine.

That concern about feeling tired later is a valid one, and I want to acknowledge that you're thinking about how this might impact your daily routine. Have you ever noticed how you feel after taking a short walk, as opposed to sitting still, even if it's just for a few minutes?
I do notice, it's like... my head feels a bit clearer, I think. But it's hard to remember that when I'm tired.

It sounds like you've noticed a difference in how you feel after taking a short walk, and that's a great insight. How might you remind yourself of that feeling of clarity when you're feeling tired and tempted to smoke?
Maybe keep a small notebook or something, and write it down, so I can remember. That way, when I'm feeling tired, I can look and think, "Yes, I did feel better that time..."

You've already had a great idea about keeping a notebook to remind yourself of how you felt after taking a short walk. Are you willing to give that a try and see if it helps you break the habit of smoking after meals or during rest?
Okay... I'll try. But if it doesn't work, I don't know what else to do...
You've taken a brave step in trying something new, and I wish you all the best on this journey to a healthier you.
Thank you... maybe this time will be different.
Now that you're willing to try keeping a notebook, have you thought about when and where you'll carry it with you, especially during those times when you're most likely to reach for a cigarette?
I think I'll keep it in my bag, so it's always with me... and maybe I can even write down some other things I'm grateful for, not just about the walks.

That's a great idea to carry the notebook in your bag and write down things you're grateful for - it sounds like a wonderful way to focus on the positive. How do you think you'll make sure you actually write in the notebook when you're feeling stressed or tempted to smoke?
I'll just try to make it a habit, like... I always wash my hands after cooking, that's a habit. Maybe writing in the notebook can be like that too.

You're thinking of creating a habit of writing in the notebook, like washing your hands after cooking. How about making it even more automatic by keeping a small pen or pencil in a place where you'll see it often, like next to your coffee mug or on your kitchen counter?
That's a good idea... it's always there, so I'll see it and remember to write. My grandson likes to play with the pens, I'll just keep one of them in a safe place.
Now that you've found a safe place to keep the pen, how about carrying a small notebook and pen with you during those afternoon rest times when you said you're often tempted to smoke?

It sounds like your relationship with your wife is a motivating factor for you to quit smoking, and her requests make you want to stop. 

Are you open to removing the cigarettes, lighters, and ashtrays from your home environment while we work together to quit smoking?

You're right, quitting drinking and quitting smoking at the same time is a big ask, and it might be better to tackle one addiction at a time. How willing would you be to try reducing the amount you drink on weekends to see if that helps reduce your urge to smoke?

Let's start by trying to find something that can replace cigarettes and drinking as a habit for you, like a new hobby or a fun activity to do on weekends.

You're already thinking of activities that could help you shift away from cigarettes and drinking. Would you be willing to try playing guitar again as a way to occupy your time on weekends, and see if that can help reduce your urge to smoke and drink?
